MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Tanner stages in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult

B) First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2)

C) Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4

D) >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measurement

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Thelarche in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4

B) First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2)

C) >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measurement

D) PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Menarche in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measurement

B) PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche

C) Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4

D) DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Testicular volume in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche

B) DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs

C) HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex steroids

D) >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measurement

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Pubic hair in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche

B) DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs

C) HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex steroids

D) Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Adrenarche in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex steroids

B) DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs

C) Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4)

D) X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constitutional delay

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Gonadarche in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4)

B) X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constitutional delay

C) HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex steroids

D) Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Peak height velocity in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constitutional delay

B) Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche

C) 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult

D) Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4)

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Bone age in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constitutional delay

B) Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche

C) 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult

D) First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Sequence (girls) in Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

A) 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult

B) Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche

C) First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2)

D) Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4

Answer: B

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Tanner stages? A) First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2 B) Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4 C) >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measureme D) 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Thelarche? A) Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4 B) >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measureme C) PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche D) First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2 Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Menarche? A) >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measureme B) PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche C) DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs D) Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4 Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Testicular volume? A) PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche B) DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs C) HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex stero D) >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measureme Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Pubic hair? A) DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs B) HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex stero C) Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4) D) PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Adrenarche? A) HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex stero B) Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4) C) X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constit D) DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Gonadarche? A) Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4) B) X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constit C) Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche D) HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex stero Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Peak height velocity? A) X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constit B) Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche C) 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult D) Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4) Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Bone age? A) Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche B) 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult C) First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2 D) X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constit Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Normal puberty and pubertal staging. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Sequence (girls)? A) 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult B) First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2 C) Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4 D) Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche Answer: D

Flashcards

  • Q: Tanner stages?

A: 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult

  • Q: Thelarche?

A: First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2)

  • Q: Menarche?

A: Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4

  • Q: Testicular volume?

A: >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measurement

  • Q: Pubic hair?

A: PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche

  • Q: Adrenarche?

A: DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs

  • Q: Gonadarche?

A: HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex steroids

  • Q: Peak height velocity?

A: Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4)

Answer Key with Explanations

  6. B - Adrenarche: DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs
  7. C - Gonadarche: HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex stero
  8. D - Peak height velocity: Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4)
  9. A - Bone age: X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constit
  10. B - Sequence (girls): Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche

  7. D - Gonadarche: HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex stero

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 31.11.3 Precocious Puberty

Definition

Precocious puberty is the onset of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in girls or 9 in boys. It is classified as central (GnRH-dependent, true precocious puberty - premature HPG activation) or peripheral (GnRH-independent, false - sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous). Central is more common in girls (often idiopathic) and far more common in boys (CNS lesion until proven otherwise - hypothalamic hamartoma, glioma). Investigations: bone age (advanced), LH/FSH, oestradiol/testosterone, 17-OH progesterone (CAH), GnRH stimulation test, MRI brain. Treatment depends on cause - GnRH analogues for central, treat underlying cause for peripheral.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Secondary sexual characteristics <8 yrs (girls) or <9 yrs (boys)

Central (true)

GnRH-dependent; premature HPG activation; idiopathic commonest in girls

Peripheral (false)

GnRH-independent; sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous

Boys with precocious

CNS lesion until proven otherwise - MRI brain mandatory

Hypothalamic hamartoma

Classic cause; gelastic seizures + central precocious puberty

CAH (congenital adrenal hyperplasia)

21-hydroxylase deficiency; virilisation in girls, premature in boys

McCune-Albright

Peripheral precocious puberty + cafe-au-lait + polyostotic fibrous dysplasia

GnRH stimulation test

Central: LH rises >5 IU/L; Peripheral: no response

Treatment central

GnRH analogues (leuprorelin, triptorelin) - monthly/3-monthly IM/SC

Treatment peripheral

Treat underlying cause: CAH -> hydrocortisone; tumour -> surgery

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionSecondary sexual characteristics <8 yrs (girls) or <9 yrs (boys)
Central (true)GnRH-dependent; premature HPG activation; idiopathic commonest in girls
Peripheral (false)GnRH-independent; sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous
Boys with precociousCNS lesion until proven otherwise - MRI brain mandatory
Hypothalamic hamartomaClassic cause; gelastic seizures + central precocious puberty
CAH (congenital adrenal hyperplasia)21-hydroxylase deficiency; virilisation in girls, premature in boys
McCune-AlbrightPeripheral precocious puberty + cafe-au-lait + polyostotic fibrous dysplasia
GnRH stimulation testCentral: LH rises >5 IU/L; Peripheral: no response
Treatment centralGnRH analogues (leuprorelin, triptorelin) - monthly/3-monthly IM/SC
Treatment peripheralTreat underlying cause: CAH -> hydrocortisone; tumour -> surgery

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Precocious puberty.

Precocious puberty is the onset of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in girls or 9 in boys. It is classified as central (GnRH-dependent, true precocious puberty - premature HPG activation) or peripheral (GnRH-independent, false - sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous). Central is more common in girls (often idiopathic) and far more common in boys (CNS lesion until proven otherwise - hypothalamic hamartoma, glioma). Investigations: bone age (advanced), LH/FSH, oestradiol/testosterone, 17-OH progesterone (CAH), GnRH stimulation test, MRI brain. Treatment depends on cause - GnRH analogues for central, treat underlying cause for peripheral.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Precocious puberty?

Definition: Secondary sexual characteristics <8 yrs (girls) or <9 yrs (boys). Central (true): GnRH-dependent; premature HPG activation; idiopathic commonest in girls. Peripheral (false): GnRH-independent; sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Precocious puberty recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Boys with precocious - CNS lesion until proven otherwise - MRI brain mandatory; Hypothalamic hamartoma - Classic cause; gelastic seizures + central precocious puberty. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Precocious puberty?

Investigations include: CAH (congenital adrenal hyperplasia) - 21-hydroxylase deficiency; virilisation in girls, premature in boys; McCune-Albright - Peripheral precocious puberty + cafe-au-lait + polyostotic fibrous dysplasia. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Precocious puberty?

First-line management: GnRH stimulation test - Central: LH rises >5 IU/L; Peripheral: no response; Treatment central - GnRH analogues (leuprorelin, triptorelin) - monthly/3-monthly IM/SC. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Precocious puberty?

Red flags and complications: Treatment peripheral - Treat underlying cause: CAH -> hydrocortisone; tumour -> surgery. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Precocious puberty - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

Mind Map

Precocious puberty
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Recognition
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Examination
├── Investigation
├── Management
│   ├── Acute
│   └── Long-term
└── Complications
    ├── Short-term
    └── Long-term

StepAction
1. RecognitionIdentify key features
2. AssessmentHEEADSSS psychosocial screen + focused exam
3. AcuteStabilise; treat emergencies; safeguarding
4. Long-termMultidisciplinary team; transition planning
5. MonitorMental health, adherence, growth, puberty

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Treatment central in Precocious puberty?

A) GnRH analogues (leuprorelin, triptorelin) - monthly/3-monthly IM/SC

B) Treat underlying cause: CAH -> hydrocortisone; tumour -> surgery

C) Secondary sexual characteristics <8 yrs (girls) or <9 yrs (boys)

A: Peripheral precocious puberty + cafe-au-lait + polyostotic fibrous dysplasia

  • Q: GnRH stimulation test?

A: Central: LH rises >5 IU/L; Peripheral: no response

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 31.11.4 Short Stature And Growth Disorders

Definition

Short stature is defined as height <2nd centile for age and sex, or >2 SD below the mean, or a height centile substantially below mid-parental height. Causes include familial short stature, constitutional delay of growth and puberty, endocrine (GH deficiency, hypothyroidism, Cushing), chronic disease (coeliac, IBD, CKD, cardiac), chromosomal (Turner), and skeletal dysplasias. Evaluation includes detailed history, growth-chart plotting, bone age, parental heights, bloods (FBC, TFTs, IGF-1, coeliac serology, karyotype in girls), and imaging. GH therapy is indicated for proven GH deficiency and specific syndromes (Turner, Prader-Willi, chronic renal failure, SGA without catch-up).

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Height <2nd centile or >2 SD below mean

Most common causes

Familial short stature + constitutional delay (normal variants)

Pathological causes

Endocrine (GH def, hypothyroid, Cushing), chronic disease, Turner, skeletal dysplasia

Mid-parental height

Boys: (dad+ mum +13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm; Girls: (mum + dad -13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm

Bone age

Delayed in CDGP, GH deficiency, hypothyroid; normal in familial

Investigations

FBC, ferritin, TFTs, IGF-1, coeliac serology, karyotype (girls), urinary organic acids

GH stimulation test

Clonidine, glucagon or insulin tolerance test; peak GH <7 ug/L = deficiency

GH therapy

Daily SC injection; indicated in GH def, Turner, Prader-Willi, SGA, CRF

Side effects GH

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension, slipped epiphyses, hyperglycaemia

Tall stature

Causes: familial, Klinefelter, Marfan, Sotos; consider if >2 SD above mean

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionHeight <2nd centile or >2 SD below mean
Most common causesFamilial short stature + constitutional delay (normal variants)
Pathological causesEndocrine (GH def, hypothyroid, Cushing), chronic disease, Turner, skeletal dysplasia
Mid-parental heightBoys: (dad+ mum +13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm; Girls: (mum + dad -13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm
Bone ageDelayed in CDGP, GH deficiency, hypothyroid; normal in familial
InvestigationsFBC, ferritin, TFTs, IGF-1, coeliac serology, karyotype (girls), urinary organic acids
GH stimulation testClonidine, glucagon or insulin tolerance test; peak GH <7 ug/L = deficiency
GH therapyDaily SC injection; indicated in GH def, Turner, Prader-Willi, SGA, CRF
Side effects GHIdiopathic intracranial hypertension, slipped epiphyses, hyperglycaemia
Tall statureCauses: familial, Klinefelter, Marfan, Sotos; consider if >2 SD above mean

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Short stature and growth disorders.

Short stature is defined as height <2nd centile for age and sex, or >2 SD below the mean, or a height centile substantially below mid-parental height. Causes include familial short stature, constitutional delay of growth and puberty, endocrine (GH deficiency, hypothyroidism, Cushing), chronic disease (coeliac, IBD, CKD, cardiac), chromosomal (Turner), and skeletal dysplasias. Evaluation includes detailed history, growth-chart plotting, bone age, parental heights, bloods (FBC, TFTs, IGF-1, coeliac serology, karyotype in girls), and imaging. GH therapy is indicated for proven GH deficiency and specific syndromes (Turner, Prader-Willi, chronic renal failure, SGA without catch-up).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Short stature and growth disorders?

Definition: Height <2nd centile or >2 SD below mean. Most common causes: Familial short stature + constitutional delay (normal variants). Pathological causes: Endocrine (GH def, hypothyroid, Cushing), chronic disease, Turner, skeletal dysplasia. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Short stature and growth disorders recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Mid-parental height - Boys: (dad+ mum +13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm; Girls: (mum + dad -13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm; Bone age - Delayed in CDGP, GH deficiency, hypothyroid; normal in familial. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Short stature and growth disorders?

Investigations include: Investigations - FBC, ferritin, TFTs, IGF-1, coeliac serology, karyotype (girls), urinary organic acids; GH stimulation test - Clonidine, glucagon or insulin tolerance test; peak GH <7 ug/L = deficiency. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Short stature and growth disorders?

First-line management: GH therapy - Daily SC injection; indicated in GH def, Turner, Prader-Willi, SGA, CRF; Side effects GH - Idiopathic intracranial hypertension, slipped epiphyses, hyperglycaemia. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Short stature and growth disorders?

Red flags and complications: Tall stature - Causes: familial, Klinefelter, Marfan, Sotos; consider if >2 SD above mean. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Short stature and growth disorders - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 31.12 Sexual Health in Adolescents 3
▸ 31.12.1 Contraception In Adolescents

Definition

Contraception in adolescents requires confidential, individualised counselling covering efficacy, side effects, reversibility, and STI prevention. Long-acting reversible contraception (LARC) - IUS, IUD, implant - is first-line for most adolescents due to highest efficacy and adherence. Combined oral contraception (COC) is widely used; progestogen-only pill (POP) is suitable when oestrogen contraindicated. Emergency contraception (copper IUD most effective; ulipristal/levonorgestrel) within 5 days. Condoms are essential for STI prevention. UK: under-13 sexual activity mandates safeguarding referral; 13-15 assessed case-by-case.

Core Clinical Content

LARC first-line

IUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99%

Implanon/Nexplanon

Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common

IUS Mirena

Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmenorrhoea

COC

21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, mood effects

POP

Cerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-hr window

DMPA (depot)

3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh benefit

Emergency contraception

Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), levonorgestrel (3 days)

Condoms

Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal ideal

Fraser guidelines

Under-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature minor

Sexual activity <13

Mandatory safeguarding referral

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
LARC first-lineIUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99%
Implanon/NexplanonSubdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common
IUS MirenaLevonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmenorrhoea
COC21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, mood effects
POPCerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-hr window
DMPA (depot)3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh benefit
Emergency contraceptionCopper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), levonorgestrel (3 days)
CondomsEssential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal ideal
Fraser guidelinesUnder-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature minor
Sexual activity <13Mandatory safeguarding referral

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Contraception in adolescents.

Contraception in adolescents requires confidential, individualised counselling covering efficacy, side effects, reversibility, and STI prevention. Long-acting reversible contraception (LARC) - IUS, IUD, implant - is first-line for most adolescents due to highest efficacy and adherence. Combined oral contraception (COC) is widely used; progestogen-only pill (POP) is suitable when oestrogen contraindicated. Emergency contraception (copper IUD most effective; ulipristal/levonorgestrel) within 5 days. Condoms are essential for STI prevention. UK: under-13 sexual activity mandates safeguarding referral; 13-15 assessed case-by-case.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Contraception in adolescents?

LARC first-line: IUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99%. Implanon/Nexplanon: Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common. IUS Mirena: Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmenorrhoea. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Contraception in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: COC - 21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, mood effects; POP - Cerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-hr window. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Contraception in adolescents?

Investigations include: DMPA (depot) - 3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh benefit; Emergency contraception - Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), levonorgestrel (3 days). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Contraception in adolescents?

First-line management: Condoms - Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal ideal; Fraser guidelines - Under-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature minor. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Contraception in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Sexual activity <13 - Mandatory safeguarding referral. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Contraception in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

C) Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal ideal

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Fraser guidelines in Contraception in adolescents?

D) Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Sexual activity <13 in Contraception in adolescents?

B) Mandatory safeguarding referral

C) Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common

D) Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmenorrhoea

Answer: B

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding LARC first-line? A) Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common B) Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmeno C) 21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, D) IUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99% Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Implanon/Nexplanon? A) Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmeno B) 21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, C) Cerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-h D) Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding IUS Mirena? A) 21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, B) Cerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-h C) 3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh b D) Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmeno Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding COC? A) Cerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-h B) 3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh b C) Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), le D) 21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding POP? A) 3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh b B) Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), le C) Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal idea D) Cerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-h Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding DMPA (depot)? A) Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), le B) Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal idea C) Under-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature D) 3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh b Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Emergency contraception? A) Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal idea B) Under-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature C) Mandatory safeguarding referral D) Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), le Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Condoms? A) Under-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature B) Mandatory safeguarding referral C) IUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99% D) Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal idea Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Fraser guidelines? A) Mandatory safeguarding referral B) IUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99% C) Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common D) Under-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Contraception in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Sexual activity <13? A) IUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99% B) Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common C) Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmeno D) Mandatory safeguarding referral Answer: D

Flashcards

  • Q: LARC first-line?

A: IUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99%

  • Q: Implanon/Nexplanon?

A: Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common

  • Q: IUS Mirena?

A: Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmenorrhoea

  • Q: COC?

A: 21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, mood effects

  • Q: POP?

A: Cerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-hr window

  • Q: DMPA (depot)?

A: 3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh benefit

  • Q: Emergency contraception?

A: Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), levonorgestrel (3 days)

  • Q: Condoms?

A: Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal ideal

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  7. C - Emergency contraception: Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), le

  9. A - Fraser guidelines: Under-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature
  10. B - Sexual activity <13: Mandatory safeguarding referral

▸ 31.12.2 Sexual Health And Gender Identity

Definition

Adolescent sexual and gender identity development is a normal part of adolescence, with sexual orientation (who one is attracted to), gender identity (ones sense of self as male, female, both, neither) and gender expression (how one presents) evolving through adolescence. LGBT+ adolescents have higher rates of mental health problems, self-harm, substance use, and homelessness due to stigma and minority stress. Gender dysphoria requires specialist multidisciplinary input (CAMHS, endocrinology for puberty suppression, surgical). All adolescents need a safe, confidential space to discuss identity.

Core Clinical Content

Sexual orientation

Lesbian, gay, bisexual, queer (LGBQ); usually emerges mid-adolescence

Gender identity

Internal sense; distinct from assigned sex at birth

Transgender

Gender identity differs from assigned sex; may experience dysphoria

Non-binary

Gender identity outside male/female binary

Coming out

Process of disclosing identity; varies; may be safe or risky

Minority stress

Stigma, discrimination, rejection drive higher mental health risk

Mental health

LGBTQ+ 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide

Puberty suppression

GnRH analogues (triptorelin) for adolescents with persistent gender dysphoria

CAMHS

Child and adolescent mental health service; specialist gender clinics (GIDS in UK)

Supportive care

Affirming name/pronouns, family acceptance, school support - improves outcomes

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Sexual orientationLesbian, gay, bisexual, queer (LGBQ); usually emerges mid-adolescence
Gender identityInternal sense; distinct from assigned sex at birth
TransgenderGender identity differs from assigned sex; may experience dysphoria
Non-binaryGender identity outside male/female binary
Coming outProcess of disclosing identity; varies; may be safe or risky
Minority stressStigma, discrimination, rejection drive higher mental health risk
Mental healthLGBTQ+ 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide
Puberty suppressionGnRH analogues (triptorelin) for adolescents with persistent gender dysphoria
CAMHSChild and adolescent mental health service; specialist gender clinics (GIDS in UK)
Supportive careAffirming name/pronouns, family acceptance, school support - improves outcomes

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Sexual health and gender identity.

Adolescent sexual and gender identity development is a normal part of adolescence, with sexual orientation (who one is attracted to), gender identity (ones sense of self as male, female, both, neither) and gender expression (how one presents) evolving through adolescence. LGBT+ adolescents have higher rates of mental health problems, self-harm, substance use, and homelessness due to stigma and minority stress. Gender dysphoria requires specialist multidisciplinary input (CAMHS, endocrinology for puberty suppression, surgical). All adolescents need a safe, confidential space to discuss identity.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Sexual health and gender identity?

Sexual orientation: Lesbian, gay, bisexual, queer (LGBQ); usually emerges mid-adolescence. Gender identity: Internal sense; distinct from assigned sex at birth. Transgender: Gender identity differs from assigned sex; may experience dysphoria. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Sexual health and gender identity recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Non-binary - Gender identity outside male/female binary; Coming out - Process of disclosing identity; varies; may be safe or risky. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Sexual health and gender identity?

Investigations include: Minority stress - Stigma, discrimination, rejection drive higher mental health risk; Mental health - LGBTQ+ 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Sexual health and gender identity?

First-line management: Puberty suppression - GnRH analogues (triptorelin) for adolescents with persistent gender dysphoria; CAMHS - Child and adolescent mental health service; specialist gender clinics (GIDS in UK). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Sexual health and gender identity?

Red flags and complications: Supportive care - Affirming name/pronouns, family acceptance, school support - improves outcomes. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sexual health and gender identity - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

Mind Map

Sexual health and gender identity
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Recognition
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Examination
├── Investigation
├── Management

│   └── Long-term

StepAction
1. RecognitionIdentify key features
2. AssessmentHEEADSSS psychosocial screen + focused exam
3. AcuteStabilise; treat emergencies; safeguarding
4. Long-termMultidisciplinary team; transition planning
5. MonitorMental health, adherence, growth, puberty

▸ 31.12.3 Sexually Transmitted Infections In Adolescents

Definition

Adolescents (15-24 yrs) have the highest rates of STIs in the UK and globally, accounting for ~50% of new infections. Biological vulnerability (cervical ectopy, immune naivety), behavioural factors (multiple partners, inconsistent condom use, substance use), and barriers to testing all contribute. Common STIs: chlamydia, gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis (rising), HIV. Screening: chlamydia/gonorrhoea NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal swab), HIV, syphilis serology. Treatment: single-dose antibiotics for chlamydia (azithromycin 1g) or 7-day doxycycline; partner notification; vaccination (HPV, Hep B, HAV).

Core Clinical Content

STI rates

50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds

Common STIs

Chlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas

Asymptomatic chlamydia

Up to 70% in females; 50% in males

Complications

PID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis

Screening

Annual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active

Chlamydia treatment

Doxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat)

Gonorrhoea treatment

Ceftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual)

Partner notification

Trace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas

HPV vaccine

School-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical cancer

HIV PrEP

Pre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
STI rates50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds
Common STIsChlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas
Asymptomatic chlamydiaUp to 70% in females; 50% in males
ComplicationsPID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis
ScreeningAnnual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active
Chlamydia treatmentDoxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat)
Gonorrhoea treatmentCeftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual)
Partner notificationTrace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas
HPV vaccineSchool-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical cancer
HIV PrEPPre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents.

Adolescents (15-24 yrs) have the highest rates of STIs in the UK and globally, accounting for ~50% of new infections. Biological vulnerability (cervical ectopy, immune naivety), behavioural factors (multiple partners, inconsistent condom use, substance use), and barriers to testing all contribute. Common STIs: chlamydia, gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis (rising), HIV. Screening: chlamydia/gonorrhoea NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal swab), HIV, syphilis serology. Treatment: single-dose antibiotics for chlamydia (azithromycin 1g) or 7-day doxycycline; partner notification; vaccination (HPV, Hep B, HAV).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

STI rates: 50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds. Common STIs: Chlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas. Asymptomatic chlamydia: Up to 70% in females; 50% in males. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Complications - PID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis; Screening - Annual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

Investigations include: Chlamydia treatment - Doxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat); Gonorrhoea treatment - Ceftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

First-line management: Partner notification - Trace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas; HPV vaccine - School-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical cancer. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: HIV PrEP - Pre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

Mind Map

Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents
├── Definition & Core Concept

STI rates?

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true regarding STI rates in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) 50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds

B) Chlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas

C) Up to 70% in females; 50% in males

D) PID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Common STIs in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) Up to 70% in females; 50% in males

B) Chlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas

C) PID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis

D) Annual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Asymptomatic chlamydia in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) PID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis

B) Annual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active

C) Up to 70% in females; 50% in males

D) Doxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat)

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Complications in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) Annual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active

B) Doxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat)

C) Ceftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual)

D) PID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Screening in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) Annual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active

B) Doxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat)

C) Ceftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual)

D) Trace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Chlamydia treatment in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) Ceftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual)

B) Doxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat)

C) Trace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas

D) School-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical canc

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Gonorrhoea treatment in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) Trace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas

B) School-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical canc

C) Ceftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual)

D) Pre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Partner notification in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) School-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical canc

B) Pre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine

C) 50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds

D) Trace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding HPV vaccine in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) School-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical cancer

B) Pre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine

C) 50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds

D) Chlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding HIV PrEP in Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

A) 50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds

B) Pre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine

C) Chlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas

D) Up to 70% in females; 50% in males

Answer: B

A: Ceftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual)

A: Trace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas

▸ 31.13 Special Adolescent Populations 2
▸ 31.13.1 Adolescent Sexual And Gender Minority Health

Definition

LGBTQ+ (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer/questioning, +) adolescents experience unique health disparities driven by minority stress, family rejection, and discrimination. They have 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts; higher rates of substance use, homelessness, and victimisation. Protective factors: family acceptance, school support, affirming healthcare. Gender dysphoria requires specialist input; puberty suppression, cross-sex hormones, and surgery are staged interventions. Mental health support is core to care, not optional.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+

Mental health

2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts

Substance use

Higher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping

Homelessness

20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection)

Family acceptance

Strongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x

Affirming care

Use chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training

Gender dysphoria

Distress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input

Puberty suppression

GnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible

Cross-sex hormones

From ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys

Surgery

Genital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+
Mental health2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts
Substance useHigher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping
Homelessness20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection)
Family acceptanceStrongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x
Affirming careUse chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training
Gender dysphoriaDistress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input
Puberty suppressionGnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible
Cross-sex hormonesFrom ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys
SurgeryGenital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Adolescent sexual and gender minority health.

LGBTQ+ (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer/questioning, +) adolescents experience unique health disparities driven by minority stress, family rejection, and discrimination. They have 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts; higher rates of substance use, homelessness, and victimisation. Protective factors: family acceptance, school support, affirming healthcare. Gender dysphoria requires specialist input; puberty suppression, cross-sex hormones, and surgery are staged interventions. Mental health support is core to care, not optional.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

Prevalence: 5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+. Mental health: 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts. Substance use: Higher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Adolescent sexual and gender minority health recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Homelessness - 20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection); Family acceptance - Strongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

Investigations include: Affirming care - Use chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training; Gender dysphoria - Distress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

First-line management: Puberty suppression - GnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible; Cross-sex hormones - From ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

Red flags and complications: Surgery - Genital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adolescent sexual and gender minority health - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Prevalence in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) 5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+

B) 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts

C) Higher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping

D) 20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Mental health in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) Higher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping

B) 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts

C) 20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection)

D) Strongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Substance use in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) 20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection)

B) Strongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x

C) Higher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping

D) Use chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Homelessness in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) Strongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x

B) Use chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training

C) Distress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input

D) 20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection)

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Family acceptance in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) Strongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x

B) Use chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training

C) Distress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input

D) GnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Affirming care in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) Distress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input

B) Use chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training

C) GnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible

D) From ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Gender dysphoria in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) GnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible

B) From ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys

C) Distress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input

D) Genital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Puberty suppression in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) From ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys

B) Genital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier

C) 5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+

D) GnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Cross-sex hormones in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) From ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys

B) Genital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier

C) 5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+

D) 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Surgery in Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

A) 5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+

B) Genital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier

C) 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts

D) Higher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping

Answer: B

▸ 31.13.2 Adolescents With Disability And Chronic Illness

Definition

Adolescents with physical, learning, or developmental disabilities face higher rates of health problems, mental health issues, social isolation, and barriers to healthcare. They may have age-specific concerns about puberty (period management, fertility, sexual function), transition, education (EHCP in UK), employment, independent living, and social relationships. Care is multidisciplinary (paediatrician, GP, OT, physio, SALT, social work, special educational needs, mental health). Reasonable adjustments (Equality Act 2010) are mandatory. Transition to adult services is often more complex and starts earlier.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

7-10% of adolescents have a disability

Categories

Physical (CP, spina bifida), sensory (visual, hearing), learning (ID, ASD), neuro (epilepsy)

Puberty

Delayed or precocious in some; period management, fertility, sexual function

Mental health

Higher rates of depression, anxiety, social isolation; screening essential

EHCP (UK)

Education, Health and Care Plan; 0-25 yrs; legal framework

Reasonable adjustments

Equality Act 2010; accessible premises, communication, longer appointments

Mental Capacity Act

16+; assume capacity; supported decision-making

Transition

More complex; adult disability services, social care, employment support

Carers

Parents/carers need support; young carer assessment; respite

Sexual health

Often overlooked; Fraser principles still apply; contraception, STI prevention

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence7-10% of adolescents have a disability
CategoriesPhysical (CP, spina bifida), sensory (visual, hearing), learning (ID, ASD), neuro (epilepsy)
PubertyDelayed or precocious in some; period management, fertility, sexual function
Mental healthHigher rates of depression, anxiety, social isolation; screening essential
EHCP (UK)Education, Health and Care Plan; 0-25 yrs; legal framework
Reasonable adjustmentsEquality Act 2010; accessible premises, communication, longer appointments
Mental Capacity Act16+; assume capacity; supported decision-making
TransitionMore complex; adult disability services, social care, employment support
CarersParents/carers need support; young carer assessment; respite
Sexual healthOften overlooked; Fraser principles still apply; contraception, STI prevention

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Adolescents with disability and chronic illness.

Adolescents with physical, learning, or developmental disabilities face higher rates of health problems, mental health issues, social isolation, and barriers to healthcare. They may have age-specific concerns about puberty (period management, fertility, sexual function), transition, education (EHCP in UK), employment, independent living, and social relationships. Care is multidisciplinary (paediatrician, GP, OT, physio, SALT, social work, special educational needs, mental health). Reasonable adjustments (Equality Act 2010) are mandatory. Transition to adult services is often more complex and starts earlier.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Adolescents with disability and chronic illness?

Prevalence: 7-10% of adolescents have a disability. Categories: Physical (CP, spina bifida), sensory (visual, hearing), learning (ID, ASD), neuro (epilepsy). Puberty: Delayed or precocious in some; period management, fertility, sexual function. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Adolescents with disability and chronic illness recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Mental health - Higher rates of depression, anxiety, social isolation; screening essential; EHCP (UK) - Education, Health and Care Plan; 0-25 yrs; legal framework. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Adolescents with disability and chronic illness?

Investigations include: Reasonable adjustments - Equality Act 2010; accessible premises, communication, longer appointments; Mental Capacity Act - 16+; assume capacity; supported decision-making. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Adolescents with disability and chronic illness?

First-line management: Transition - More complex; adult disability services, social care, employment support; Carers - Parents/carers need support; young carer assessment; respite. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Adolescents with disability and chronic illness?

Red flags and complications: Sexual health - Often overlooked; Fraser principles still apply; contraception, STI prevention. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adolescents with disability and chronic illness - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

▸ 31.14 Transition of Care 3
▸ 31.14.1 Models Of Transition Care

Definition

Several transition models exist, ranging from simple transfer to joint clinics to dedicated transition services. Evidence favours structured, multi-component interventions with both paediatric and adult team involvement. Joint clinics (paediatric and adult teams co-consult) reduce disengagement. Transition coordinators / navigators improve attendance and self-management. Disease-specific transition programmes (e.g., Cystic Fibrosis, Congenital Heart Disease, Diabetes, JIA) are most developed. Universal transition principles apply to all long-term conditions; service design must be flexible for less common conditions.

Core Clinical Content

Joint clinics

Paediatric + adult team co-consult; reduces disengagement; familiar faces

Transition coordinator

Named professional; supports navigation, planning, follow-up

Disease-specific programmes

CF, CHD, diabetes, JIA, IBD - most developed

Young adult clinics

Adult setting, but with developmental flexibility (e.g., 16-25 yrs)

Ready Steady Go

10-step transition programme widely used in UK paediatric services

Hello/Hands-off

Adult team gradually takes over from paediatric

Telephone/virtual follow-up

Improves engagement with hard-to-reach young adults

Peer support

Peer mentors, social media support groups, condition-specific charities

Mental health integration

Embedded CAMHS or adult mental health during transition

Evaluation

PDSA cycles; outcome measures: attendance, disease control, satisfaction

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Joint clinicsPaediatric + adult team co-consult; reduces disengagement; familiar faces
Transition coordinatorNamed professional; supports navigation, planning, follow-up
Disease-specific programmesCF, CHD, diabetes, JIA, IBD - most developed
Young adult clinicsAdult setting, but with developmental flexibility (e.g., 16-25 yrs)
Ready Steady Go10-step transition programme widely used in UK paediatric services
Hello/Hands-offAdult team gradually takes over from paediatric
Telephone/virtual follow-upImproves engagement with hard-to-reach young adults
Peer supportPeer mentors, social media support groups, condition-specific charities
Mental health integrationEmbedded CAMHS or adult mental health during transition
EvaluationPDSA cycles; outcome measures: attendance, disease control, satisfaction

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Models of transition care.

Several transition models exist, ranging from simple transfer to joint clinics to dedicated transition services. Evidence favours structured, multi-component interventions with both paediatric and adult team involvement. Joint clinics (paediatric and adult teams co-consult) reduce disengagement. Transition coordinators / navigators improve attendance and self-management. Disease-specific transition programmes (e.g., Cystic Fibrosis, Congenital Heart Disease, Diabetes, JIA) are most developed. Universal transition principles apply to all long-term conditions; service design must be flexible for less common conditions.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Models of transition care?

Joint clinics: Paediatric + adult team co-consult; reduces disengagement; familiar faces. Transition coordinator: Named professional; supports navigation, planning, follow-up. Disease-specific programmes: CF, CHD, diabetes, JIA, IBD - most developed. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Models of transition care recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Young adult clinics - Adult setting, but with developmental flexibility (e.g., 16-25 yrs); Ready Steady Go - 10-step transition programme widely used in UK paediatric services. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Models of transition care?

Investigations include: Hello/Hands-off - Adult team gradually takes over from paediatric; Telephone/virtual follow-up - Improves engagement with hard-to-reach young adults. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Models of transition care?

First-line management: Peer support - Peer mentors, social media support groups, condition-specific charities; Mental health integration - Embedded CAMHS or adult mental health during transition. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Models of transition care?

Red flags and complications: Evaluation - PDSA cycles; outcome measures: attendance, disease control, satisfaction. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Models of transition care - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

D) Adult setting, but with developmental flexibility (e.g., 16-25 yrs)

D) 10-step transition programme widely used in UK paediatric services

D) CF, CHD, diabetes, JIA, IBD - most developed

Flashcards

  • Q: Joint clinics?

A: Paediatric + adult team co-consult; reduces disengagement; familiar faces

  • Q: Transition coordinator?

A: Named professional; supports navigation, planning, follow-up

  • Q: Disease-specific programmes?

A: CF, CHD, diabetes, JIA, IBD - most developed

  • Q: Young adult clinics?

A: Adult setting, but with developmental flexibility (e.g., 16-25 yrs)

  • Q: Ready Steady Go?

A: 10-step transition programme widely used in UK paediatric services

  • Q: Hello/Hands-off?

A: Adult team gradually takes over from paediatric

  • Q: Telephone/virtual follow-up?

A: Improves engagement with hard-to-reach young adults

  • Q: Peer support?

A: Peer mentors, social media support groups, condition-specific charities

Answer Key with Explanations

  6. B - Hello/Hands-off: Adult team gradually takes over from paediatric
  7. C - Telephone/virtual follow-up: Improves engagement with hard-to-reach young adults
  8. D - Peer support: Peer mentors, social media support groups, condition-specifi
  9. A - Mental health integration: Embedded CAMHS or adult mental health during transition
  10. B - Evaluation: PDSA cycles; outcome measures: attendance, disease control,

▸ 31.14.2 Transition Of Care Principles

Definition

Transition is the purposeful, planned movement of adolescents and young adults with chronic conditions from child-centred to adult-centred healthcare. Distinct from transfer (the actual event of moving), transition is a multi-year process. Failures in transition are associated with disengagement, non-adherence, emergency admissions and increased morbidity. National guidance (NICE 2016, Got Transition, AAP/AAFP/ACP consensus 2011) outlines 6 core elements: transition policy, tracking, readiness assessment, planning, transfer of care, and completion. Transition should be developmentally appropriate, coordinated, and start in early adolescence (12-14 yrs).

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Planned, purposeful process from child- to adult-centred care

Transition vs transfer

Transition = process; Transfer = event

6 core elements

Policy, Tracking, Readiness assessment, Planning, Transfer, Completion

NICE NG43 (2016)

UK guidance; transition planning from age 13-14

Got Transition

US six-core-elements framework; widely used

Timing

Start planning ~12-14 yrs; transfer typically 16-19 yrs; flexible

Outcome data

Poor transition -> non-adherence, disengagement, ED visits, morbidity

Multidisciplinary

Paediatric + adult teams + GP + adolescent + family

Named worker

Transition coordinator helps navigation; improves outcomes

Quality indicators

Successful transfer, attendance at adult clinic, disease control, satisfaction

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPlanned, purposeful process from child- to adult-centred care
Transition vs transferTransition = process; Transfer = event
6 core elementsPolicy, Tracking, Readiness assessment, Planning, Transfer, Completion
NICE NG43 (2016)UK guidance; transition planning from age 13-14
Got TransitionUS six-core-elements framework; widely used
TimingStart planning ~12-14 yrs; transfer typically 16-19 yrs; flexible
Outcome dataPoor transition -> non-adherence, disengagement, ED visits, morbidity
MultidisciplinaryPaediatric + adult teams + GP + adolescent + family
Named workerTransition coordinator helps navigation; improves outcomes
Quality indicatorsSuccessful transfer, attendance at adult clinic, disease control, satisfaction

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Transition of care principles.

Transition is the purposeful, planned movement of adolescents and young adults with chronic conditions from child-centred to adult-centred healthcare. Distinct from transfer (the actual event of moving), transition is a multi-year process. Failures in transition are associated with disengagement, non-adherence, emergency admissions and increased morbidity. National guidance (NICE 2016, Got Transition, AAP/AAFP/ACP consensus 2011) outlines 6 core elements: transition policy, tracking, readiness assessment, planning, transfer of care, and completion. Transition should be developmentally appropriate, coordinated, and start in early adolescence (12-14 yrs).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Transition of care principles?

Definition: Planned, purposeful process from child- to adult-centred care. Transition vs transfer: Transition = process; Transfer = event. 6 core elements: Policy, Tracking, Readiness assessment, Planning, Transfer, Completion. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Transition of care principles recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: NICE NG43 (2016) - UK guidance; transition planning from age 13-14; Got Transition - US six-core-elements framework; widely used. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Transition of care principles?

Investigations include: Timing - Start planning ~12-14 yrs; transfer typically 16-19 yrs; flexible; Outcome data - Poor transition -> non-adherence, disengagement, ED visits, morbidity. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Transition of care principles?

First-line management: Multidisciplinary - Paediatric + adult teams + GP + adolescent + family; Named worker - Transition coordinator helps navigation; improves outcomes. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Transition of care principles?

Red flags and complications: Quality indicators - Successful transfer, attendance at adult clinic, disease control, satisfaction. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Transition of care principles - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

Mind Map

Transition of care principles
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Recognition
│   ├── Presentation

├── Management

│   └── Long-term
└── Complications
    ├── Short-term
    └── Long-term

StepAction
1. RecognitionIdentify key features
2. AssessmentHEEADSSS psychosocial screen + focused exam
3. AcuteStabilise; treat emergencies; safeguarding
4. Long-termMultidisciplinary team; transition planning
5. MonitorMental health, adherence, growth, puberty

▸ 31.14.3 Transition Readiness Assessment

Definition

Transition readiness assessment evaluates the adolescents knowledge, skills, and self-management behaviours needed for adult healthcare. Validated tools include the STARx (Self-Management and Transition to Adulthood Questionnaire), Am I ON TRAC, and Transition Readiness Assessment Questionnaire (TRAQ). Domains: disease knowledge, medication management, communication with providers, navigation of healthcare, self-advocacy, daily living skills. Assessment should start in early adolescence and repeat over time, informing transition planning. Health literacy, executive function, neurodevelopmental conditions (autism, ADHD) and learning disability need to be considered.

Core Clinical Content

Tools

STARx, TRAQ, Am I ON TRAC, Ready Steady Go

Domains

Disease knowledge, medication, communication, navigation, self-advocacy, daily living

Timing

Start at 12-14; repeat at 14-16, 16-18

STARx

Self-Management and Transition to Adulthood with Rx = medication domain

Health literacy

Understanding of own condition, treatments, when to seek help

Executive function

Planning, organisation, problem-solving; assess in ADHD, learning disability

Self-advocacy

Ability to ask questions, raise concerns, express preferences

Communication

Talking to clinicians alone, telephone triage, asking about results

Practical

Own medications, prescription refills, appointment booking, transport

Reasonable adjustments

Mental capacity assessment, easy-read materials, advocates

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ToolsSTARx, TRAQ, Am I ON TRAC, Ready Steady Go
DomainsDisease knowledge, medication, communication, navigation, self-advocacy, daily living
TimingStart at 12-14; repeat at 14-16, 16-18
STARxSelf-Management and Transition to Adulthood with Rx = medication domain
Health literacyUnderstanding of own condition, treatments, when to seek help
Executive functionPlanning, organisation, problem-solving; assess in ADHD, learning disability
Self-advocacyAbility to ask questions, raise concerns, express preferences
CommunicationTalking to clinicians alone, telephone triage, asking about results
PracticalOwn medications, prescription refills, appointment booking, transport
Reasonable adjustmentsMental capacity assessment, easy-read materials, advocates

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Transition readiness assessment.

Transition readiness assessment evaluates the adolescents knowledge, skills, and self-management behaviours needed for adult healthcare. Validated tools include the STARx (Self-Management and Transition to Adulthood Questionnaire), Am I ON TRAC, and Transition Readiness Assessment Questionnaire (TRAQ). Domains: disease knowledge, medication management, communication with providers, navigation of healthcare, self-advocacy, daily living skills. Assessment should start in early adolescence and repeat over time, informing transition planning. Health literacy, executive function, neurodevelopmental conditions (autism, ADHD) and learning disability need to be considered.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Transition readiness assessment?

Tools: STARx, TRAQ, Am I ON TRAC, Ready Steady Go. Domains: Disease knowledge, medication, communication, navigation, self-advocacy, daily living. Timing: Start at 12-14; repeat at 14-16, 16-18. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Transition readiness assessment recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: STARx - Self-Management and Transition to Adulthood with Rx = medication domain; Health literacy - Understanding of own condition, treatments, when to seek help. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Transition readiness assessment?

Investigations include: Executive function - Planning, organisation, problem-solving; assess in ADHD, learning disability; Self-advocacy - Ability to ask questions, raise concerns, express preferences. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Transition readiness assessment?

First-line management: Communication - Talking to clinicians alone, telephone triage, asking about results; Practical - Own medications, prescription refills, appointment booking, transport. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Transition readiness assessment?

Red flags and complications: Reasonable adjustments - Mental capacity assessment, easy-read materials, advocates. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Transition readiness assessment - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)
