  6. B - Complications: Oesophagitis, gastric dilatation, arrhythmias, sudden death
  7. C - Differential AN binge-purge: AN with binge-purge: low weight; BN: normal or overweight
  8. D - CBT-E: Enhanced cognitive behavioural therapy - first-line for BN a
  9. A - Pharmacotherapy BN: Fluoxetine 60 mg (only licensed drug for adult BN)
  10. B - Prognosis: Better than AN; 50-70% recover; risk of relapse

SBAs

  1. D - BN DSM-5: Recurrent binges + compensatory behaviours, >=weekly, >=3 mo
  2. D - BED DSM-5: Recurrent binges without compensation; marked distress
  3. D - Compensatory behaviours: Self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, exercise, fasti
  4. D - Physical signs: Russell sign (knuckle calluses), parotid hypertrophy, dental
  5. D - Electrolytes: Hypokalaemia, hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis, dehydratio
  6. D - Complications: Oesophagitis, gastric dilatation, arrhythmias, sudden death
  7. D - Differential AN binge-purge: AN with binge-purge: low weight; BN: normal or overweight
  8. D - CBT-E: Enhanced cognitive behavioural therapy - first-line for BN a
  9. D - Pharmacotherapy BN: Fluoxetine 60 mg (only licensed drug for adult BN)
  10. D - Prognosis: Better than AN; 50-70% recover; risk of relapse

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 31.4.5 Self Harm And Suicide In Adolescents

Definition

Self-harm (non-suicidal self-injury, NSSI) is intentional self-poisoning or injury without suicidal intent; suicide is self-harm with intent to die. Both are leading causes of adolescent death globally - suicide is the 2nd-3rd leading cause of death in 15-24 year-olds. Risk factors: prior self-harm, mental illness (depression, BPD), substance use, ACEs, bullying, LGBT identity, easy access to means. Immediate management: medical stabilisation of injury, mental health assessment, safety planning, restricting means (especially ligature points, medications, firearms). Long-term: dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) for BPD, CBT, family therapy, school-based prevention.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Suicide 2nd-3rd leading cause of death in 15-24 yrs; F > M attempts, M > F deaths

NSSI prevalence

10-20% of adolescents; cutting, burning, hitting, scratching

Risk factors

Prior self-harm, mental illness, substance use, ACEs, bullying, LGBT, access to means

Warning signs

Giving possessions, saying goodbye, sudden calmness, talking about death

Means restriction

Remove access: ligature points, medications, firearms, pesticides

Immediate management

ABCDE medical stabilisation; mental state exam; suicide risk assessment

Risk assessment

Intent, plan, means, timeline, prior attempts, protective factors

Safety plan

Written; identifies warning signs, coping, contacts, restricted means, ED if needed

Long-term therapy

DBT for BPD; CBT for depression/anxiety; safety planning intervention (SPI)

Media reporting

Avoid detail on method; do not glorify; encourage help-seeking (Papageno effect)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologySuicide 2nd-3rd leading cause of death in 15-24 yrs; F > M attempts, M > F deaths
NSSI prevalence10-20% of adolescents; cutting, burning, hitting, scratching
Risk factorsPrior self-harm, mental illness, substance use, ACEs, bullying, LGBT, access to means
Warning signsGiving possessions, saying goodbye, sudden calmness, talking about death
Means restrictionRemove access: ligature points, medications, firearms, pesticides
Immediate managementABCDE medical stabilisation; mental state exam; suicide risk assessment
Risk assessmentIntent, plan, means, timeline, prior attempts, protective factors
Safety planWritten; identifies warning signs, coping, contacts, restricted means, ED if needed
Long-term therapyDBT for BPD; CBT for depression/anxiety; safety planning intervention (SPI)
Media reportingAvoid detail on method; do not glorify; encourage help-seeking (Papageno effect)

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Self-harm and suicide in adolescents.

Self-harm (non-suicidal self-injury, NSSI) is intentional self-poisoning or injury without suicidal intent; suicide is self-harm with intent to die. Both are leading causes of adolescent death globally - suicide is the 2nd-3rd leading cause of death in 15-24 year-olds. Risk factors: prior self-harm, mental illness (depression, BPD), substance use, ACEs, bullying, LGBT identity, easy access to means. Immediate management: medical stabilisation of injury, mental health assessment, safety planning, restricting means (especially ligature points, medications, firearms). Long-term: dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) for BPD, CBT, family therapy, school-based prevention.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Self-harm and suicide in adolescents?

Epidemiology: Suicide 2nd-3rd leading cause of death in 15-24 yrs; F > M attempts, M > F deaths. NSSI prevalence: 10-20% of adolescents; cutting, burning, hitting, scratching. Risk factors: Prior self-harm, mental illness, substance use, ACEs, bullying, LGBT, access to means. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Self-harm and suicide in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Warning signs - Giving possessions, saying goodbye, sudden calmness, talking about death; Means restriction - Remove access: ligature points, medications, firearms, pesticides. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Self-harm and suicide in adolescents?

Investigations include: Immediate management - ABCDE medical stabilisation; mental state exam; suicide risk assessment; Risk assessment - Intent, plan, means, timeline, prior attempts, protective factors. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Self-harm and suicide in adolescents?

First-line management: Safety plan - Written; identifies warning signs, coping, contacts, restricted means, ED if needed; Long-term therapy - DBT for BPD; CBT for depression/anxiety; safety planning intervention (SPI). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Self-harm and suicide in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Media reporting - Avoid detail on method; do not glorify; encourage help-seeking (Papageno effect). Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Self-harm and suicide in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

▸ 31.5 Adolescent Mental Health and Behaviour 8
▸ 31.5.1 Bipolar Disorder In Adolescents

Definition

Bipolar disorder in adolescents is characterised by episodes of elevated/irritable mood with increased energy/activity (mania/hypomania) alternating with periods of depression. DSM-5 distinguishes bipolar I (>=1 manic episode, duration >=7 days or hospitalisation) and bipolar II (>=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode). Mixed episodes and rapid cycling are common in adolescents. Prevalence 1-3% adolescents; M=F; high suicide risk (15-20x general population). Often misdiagnosed as ADHD, conduct disorder, or unipolar depression. Management: mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine), atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone), psychotherapy (CBT, family-focused, IPSRT). Comorbid ADHD/anxiety common. Prognosis: chronic relapsing course; treatment adherence a major challenge.

Core Clinical Content

Bipolar I

=1 manic episode (>=7 days or hospitalisation)

Bipolar II

=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode

Mania criteria (DIG FAST)

Distractibility, Irresponsibility, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increased, Sleep decreased, Talkativeness

Prevalence

1-3% adolescents; M=F

Suicide risk

15-20x general population; highest in mixed episodes

Misdiagnosis

Often confused with ADHD, conduct disorder, unipolar depression

First-line pharmacotherapy

Lithium (gold standard), valproate, atypical antipsychotics

Lithium monitoring

TFTs, U&E, Ca, pregnancy test; narrow therapeutic index

Lamotrigine

Effective for bipolar depression; slow titration (Stevens-Johnson risk)

Valproate

Avoid in females (teratogenicity); useful in males, severe mania

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Bipolar I>=1 manic episode (>=7 days or hospitalisation)
Bipolar II>=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode
Mania criteria (DIG FAST)Distractibility, Irresponsibility, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increased, Sleep decreased, Talkativeness
Prevalence1-3% adolescents; M=F
Suicide risk15-20x general population; highest in mixed episodes
MisdiagnosisOften confused with ADHD, conduct disorder, unipolar depression
First-line pharmacotherapyLithium (gold standard), valproate, atypical antipsychotics
Lithium monitoringTFTs, U&E, Ca, pregnancy test; narrow therapeutic index
LamotrigineEffective for bipolar depression; slow titration (Stevens-Johnson risk)
ValproateAvoid in females (teratogenicity); useful in males, severe mania

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Bipolar disorder in adolescents.

Bipolar disorder in adolescents is characterised by episodes of elevated/irritable mood with increased energy/activity (mania/hypomania) alternating with periods of depression. DSM-5 distinguishes bipolar I (>=1 manic episode, duration >=7 days or hospitalisation) and bipolar II (>=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode). Mixed episodes and rapid cycling are common in adolescents. Prevalence 1-3% adolescents; M=F; high suicide risk (15-20x general population). Often misdiagnosed as ADHD, conduct disorder, or unipolar depression. Management: mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine), atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone), psychotherapy (CBT, family-focused, IPSRT). Comorbid ADHD/anxiety common. Prognosis: chronic relapsing course; treatment adherence a major challenge.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Bipolar disorder in adolescents?

Bipolar I: >=1 manic episode (>=7 days or hospitalisation). Bipolar II: >=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode. Mania criteria (DIG FAST): Distractibility, Irresponsibility, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increased, Sleep decreased, Talkativeness. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Bipolar disorder in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Prevalence - 1-3% adolescents; M=F; Suicide risk - 15-20x general population; highest in mixed episodes. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Bipolar disorder in adolescents?

Investigations include: Misdiagnosis - Often confused with ADHD, conduct disorder, unipolar depression; First-line pharmacotherapy - Lithium (gold standard), valproate, atypical antipsychotics. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Bipolar disorder in adolescents?

First-line management: Lithium monitoring - TFTs, U&E, Ca, pregnancy test; narrow therapeutic index; Lamotrigine - Effective for bipolar depression; slow titration (Stevens-Johnson risk). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Bipolar disorder in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Valproate - Avoid in females (teratogenicity); useful in males, severe mania. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Bipolar disorder in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

B) Avoid in females (teratogenicity); useful in males, severe mania

▸ 31.5.2 Conduct Disorder And Oppositional Defiant Disorder

Definition

Conduct disorder (CD) is a persistent pattern of behaviour in adolescents that violates the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms, including aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, deceitfulness/theft, and serious rule violations. Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD) is a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, or vindictiveness lasting >=6 months, but without the serious violations of CD. CD affects 3-5% of adolescents, M:F 3:1, often comorbid with ADHD, depression, substance use. Management: multimodal — parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills training, school intervention; severe cases may need residential or forensic placement. Prognosis improves with early intervention; ~50% desist by adulthood.

Core Clinical Content

Conduct disorder DSM-5

=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, property, deceit, rules

ODD DSM-5

Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 months

Prevalence

CD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1

Comorbidity

ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disability

Risk factors

Genetics, harsh parenting, ACEs, poverty, school failure

Severity specifiers

Mild, moderate, severe; age of onset (before/after 10 yrs)

First-line therapy

Parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills, school intervention

Pharmacotherapy

Risperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid ADHD

Prognosis

50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity, criminality

Differential

ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Conduct disorder DSM-5>=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, property, deceit, rules
ODD DSM-5Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 months
PrevalenceCD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1
ComorbidityADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disability
Risk factorsGenetics, harsh parenting, ACEs, poverty, school failure
Severity specifiersMild, moderate, severe; age of onset (before/after 10 yrs)
First-line therapyParent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills, school intervention
PharmacotherapyRisperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid ADHD
Prognosis50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity, criminality
DifferentialODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder.

Conduct disorder (CD) is a persistent pattern of behaviour in adolescents that violates the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms, including aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, deceitfulness/theft, and serious rule violations. Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD) is a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, or vindictiveness lasting >=6 months, but without the serious violations of CD. CD affects 3-5% of adolescents, M:F 3:1, often comorbid with ADHD, depression, substance use. Management: multimodal — parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills training, school intervention; severe cases may need residential or forensic placement. Prognosis improves with early intervention; ~50% desist by adulthood.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder?

Conduct disorder DSM-5: >=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, property, deceit, rules. ODD DSM-5: Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 months. Prevalence: CD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Comorbidity - ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disability; Risk factors - Genetics, harsh parenting, ACEs, poverty, school failure. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder?

Investigations include: Severity specifiers - Mild, moderate, severe; age of onset (before/after 10 yrs); First-line therapy - Parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills, school intervention. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder?

First-line management: Pharmacotherapy - Risperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid ADHD; Prognosis - 50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity, criminality. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder?

Red flags and complications: Differential - ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

  1. Which of the following is true regarding First-line therapy in Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder?

B) 50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity, criminality

D) ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Pharmacotherapy in Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder?

A) 50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity, criminality

B) ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use

C) >=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, property, deceit, rules

D) Risperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid ADHD

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Prognosis in Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder?

A) 50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity, criminality

B) ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use

C) >=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, property, deceit, rules

D) Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 months

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Differential in Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder?

A) >=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, property, deceit, rules

B) ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use

C) Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 months

D) CD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1

Answer: B

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Conduct disorder DSM-5? A) Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 month B) CD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1 C) ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning dis D) >=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, propert Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding ODD DSM-5? A) CD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1 B) ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning dis C) Genetics, harsh parenting, ACEs, poverty, school failure D) Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 month Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Prevalence? A) ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning dis B) Genetics, harsh parenting, ACEs, poverty, school failure C) Mild, moderate, severe; age of onset (before/after 10 yrs) D) CD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1 Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Comorbidity? A) Genetics, harsh parenting, ACEs, poverty, school failure B) Mild, moderate, severe; age of onset (before/after 10 yrs) C) Parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills, school D) ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning dis Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Risk factors? A) Mild, moderate, severe; age of onset (before/after 10 yrs) B) Parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills, school C) Risperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid A D) Genetics, harsh parenting, ACEs, poverty, school failure Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Severity specifiers? A) Parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills, school B) Risperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid A C) 50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity D) Mild, moderate, severe; age of onset (before/after 10 yrs) Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding First-line therapy? A) Risperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid A B) 50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity C) ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use D) Parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills, school Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Pharmacotherapy? A) 50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity B) ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use C) >=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, propert D) Risperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid A Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Prognosis? A) ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use B) >=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, propert C) Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 month D) 50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Differential? A) >=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, propert B) Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 month C) CD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1 D) ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use Answer: D

Flashcards

A: >=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, property, deceit, rules

  • Q: ODD DSM-5?

A: Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 months

  • Q: Prevalence?

A: CD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1

A: ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disability

  • Q: Risk factors?

A: Genetics, harsh parenting, ACEs, poverty, school failure

A: Mild, moderate, severe; age of onset (before/after 10 yrs)

  • Q: First-line therapy?

A: Parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills, school intervention

  • Q: Pharmacotherapy?

A: Risperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid ADHD

▸ 31.5.3 Disorders Of Sex Development

Definition

Disorders of sex development (DSD) are congenital conditions in which chromosomal, gonadal, or anatomical sex development is atypical. The 2006 Chicago Consensus classifies DSD into: sex chromosome DSD (Turner 45,X; Klinefelter 47,XXY; mixed gonadal dysgenesis 45,X/46,XY), 46,XY DSD (androgen insensitivity syndrome, 5-alpha reductase deficiency, gonadal dysgenesis), and 46,XX DSD (congenital adrenal hyperplasia, aromatase deficiency, MRKH). Incidence ~1 in 4500 births. Multidisciplinary team management is essential (endocrinology, urology, psychology, genetics). Most present in neonates with ambiguous genitalia or in adolescents with primary amenorrhoea/delayed puberty. Management: sex assignment (now deferred where possible), hormone replacement, surgical timing, psychological support, disclosure counselling.

Core Clinical Content

DSD definition

Congenital conditions with atypical chromosomal/gonadal/anatomical sex development

Incidence

~1 in 4500 births

Sex chromosome DSD

Turner 45X, Klinefelter 47XXY, mixed 45X/46XY

46,XY DSD

Androgen insensitivity, 5-alpha reductase deficiency, gonadal dysgenesis

46,XX DSD

CAH (most common), aromatase deficiency, MRKH

CAH classic

21-hydroxylase deficiency; virilisation; salt-wasting (1 in 10,000)

AIS complete

46,XY female phenotype; primary amenorrhoea; testes intra-abdominal

MRKH

46,XX female; absent uterus/vagina; normal ovaries; primary amenorrhoea

MDT

Endocrinology, urology, psychology, genetics, gynaecology

Sex assignment

DSD consensus: deferred where possible; individualised

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSD definitionCongenital conditions with atypical chromosomal/gonadal/anatomical sex development
Incidence~1 in 4500 births
Sex chromosome DSDTurner 45X, Klinefelter 47XXY, mixed 45X/46XY
46,XY DSDAndrogen insensitivity, 5-alpha reductase deficiency, gonadal dysgenesis
46,XX DSDCAH (most common), aromatase deficiency, MRKH
CAH classic21-hydroxylase deficiency; virilisation; salt-wasting (1 in 10,000)
AIS complete46,XY female phenotype; primary amenorrhoea; testes intra-abdominal
MRKH46,XX female; absent uterus/vagina; normal ovaries; primary amenorrhoea
MDTEndocrinology, urology, psychology, genetics, gynaecology
Sex assignmentDSD consensus: deferred where possible; individualised

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Disorders of sex development.

Disorders of sex development (DSD) are congenital conditions in which chromosomal, gonadal, or anatomical sex development is atypical. The 2006 Chicago Consensus classifies DSD into: sex chromosome DSD (Turner 45,X; Klinefelter 47,XXY; mixed gonadal dysgenesis 45,X/46,XY), 46,XY DSD (androgen insensitivity syndrome, 5-alpha reductase deficiency, gonadal dysgenesis), and 46,XX DSD (congenital adrenal hyperplasia, aromatase deficiency, MRKH). Incidence ~1 in 4500 births. Multidisciplinary team management is essential (endocrinology, urology, psychology, genetics). Most present in neonates with ambiguous genitalia or in adolescents with primary amenorrhoea/delayed puberty. Management: sex assignment (now deferred where possible), hormone replacement, surgical timing, psychological support, disclosure counselling.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Disorders of sex development?

DSD definition: Congenital conditions with atypical chromosomal/gonadal/anatomical sex development. Incidence: ~1 in 4500 births. Sex chromosome DSD: Turner 45X, Klinefelter 47XXY, mixed 45X/46XY. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Disorders of sex development recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: 46,XY DSD - Androgen insensitivity, 5-alpha reductase deficiency, gonadal dysgenesis; 46,XX DSD - CAH (most common), aromatase deficiency, MRKH. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Disorders of sex development?

Investigations include: CAH classic - 21-hydroxylase deficiency; virilisation; salt-wasting (1 in 10,000); AIS complete - 46,XY female phenotype; primary amenorrhoea; testes intra-abdominal. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Disorders of sex development?

First-line management: MRKH - 46,XX female; absent uterus/vagina; normal ovaries; primary amenorrhoea; MDT - Endocrinology, urology, psychology, genetics, gynaecology. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Disorders of sex development?

Red flags and complications: Sex assignment - DSD consensus: deferred where possible; individualised. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Disorders of sex development - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

│   └── Long-term

▸ 31.5.4 Female Genital Mutilation

Definition

Female genital mutilation (FGM) comprises all procedures involving partial or total removal of external female genitalia or other injury to the female genital organs for non-medical reasons (WHO). Classified into 4 types (I-IV) by WHO. ~200 million girls/women in 30 countries affected, mainly Africa and Middle East; ~3.6 million at risk annually. UK practice: mandatory reporting for under-18s (FGM is a serious crime); mandatory health/education data collection. Health complications: acute (haemorrhage, infection, pain), obstetric (prolonged labour, fistula, neonatal death), psychological (PTSD, depression, anxiety), sexual (dyspareunia, sexual dysfunction). Management: deinfibulation (type III reversal) before pregnancy/vaginal delivery; multi-disciplinary team; safeguarding mandatory.

Core Clinical Content

WHO definition

All procedures removing/injuring female genitalia for non-medical reasons

WHO classification

Type I (clitoridectomy), II (excision), III (infibulation), IV (other)

Prevalence

~200 million affected; ~3.6 million at risk annually

Geographic distribution

30 countries; mainly Africa, Middle East; some Asia

UK legal

FGM Act 2003; criminal offence; mandatory reporting <18 (since 2015)

Acute complications

Haemorrhage, infection (sepsis, tetanus), pain, urinary retention

Long-term complications

Chronic pain, dyspareunia, recurrent UTI, dysmenorrhoea

Obstetric complications

Prolonged labour, fistula, perinatal death

Psychological

PTSD, depression, anxiety, sexual dysfunction

Deinfibulation

Surgical reversal of type III; before pregnancy/delivery

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
WHO definitionAll procedures removing/injuring female genitalia for non-medical reasons
WHO classificationType I (clitoridectomy), II (excision), III (infibulation), IV (other)
Prevalence~200 million affected; ~3.6 million at risk annually
Geographic distribution30 countries; mainly Africa, Middle East; some Asia
UK legalFGM Act 2003; criminal offence; mandatory reporting <18 (since 2015)
Acute complicationsHaemorrhage, infection (sepsis, tetanus), pain, urinary retention
Long-term complicationsChronic pain, dyspareunia, recurrent UTI, dysmenorrhoea
Obstetric complicationsProlonged labour, fistula, perinatal death
PsychologicalPTSD, depression, anxiety, sexual dysfunction
DeinfibulationSurgical reversal of type III; before pregnancy/delivery

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Female genital mutilation.

Female genital mutilation (FGM) comprises all procedures involving partial or total removal of external female genitalia or other injury to the female genital organs for non-medical reasons (WHO). Classified into 4 types (I-IV) by WHO. ~200 million girls/women in 30 countries affected, mainly Africa and Middle East; ~3.6 million at risk annually. UK practice: mandatory reporting for under-18s (FGM is a serious crime); mandatory health/education data collection. Health complications: acute (haemorrhage, infection, pain), obstetric (prolonged labour, fistula, neonatal death), psychological (PTSD, depression, anxiety), sexual (dyspareunia, sexual dysfunction). Management: deinfibulation (type III reversal) before pregnancy/vaginal delivery; multi-disciplinary team; safeguarding mandatory.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Female genital mutilation?

WHO definition: All procedures removing/injuring female genitalia for non-medical reasons. WHO classification: Type I (clitoridectomy), II (excision), III (infibulation), IV (other). Prevalence: ~200 million affected; ~3.6 million at risk annually. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Female genital mutilation recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Geographic distribution - 30 countries; mainly Africa, Middle East; some Asia; UK legal - FGM Act 2003; criminal offence; mandatory reporting <18 (since 2015). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Female genital mutilation?

Investigations include: Acute complications - Haemorrhage, infection (sepsis, tetanus), pain, urinary retention; Long-term complications - Chronic pain, dyspareunia, recurrent UTI, dysmenorrhoea. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Female genital mutilation?

First-line management: Obstetric complications - Prolonged labour, fistula, perinatal death; Psychological - PTSD, depression, anxiety, sexual dysfunction. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Female genital mutilation?

Red flags and complications: Deinfibulation - Surgical reversal of type III; before pregnancy/delivery. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Female genital mutilation - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 31.5.5 Gaming Disorder And Internet Addiction

Definition

Gaming disorder (ICD-11, 2018) is characterised by a pattern of persistent or recurrent gaming behaviour (digital or video gaming) of sufficient severity to result in significant impairment in personal, family, social, educational, occupational or other important areas of functioning, typically evident over at least 12 months. Internet addiction (broader concept) is not in ICD-11 but includes compulsive internet use affecting school, social and family life. Prevalence: 1-3% adolescents; M:F 3:1; peak 12-18 yrs. Often comorbid with ADHD, anxiety, depression, ASD. Management: CBT (often family-based), motivational interviewing, screen-time contracts, school reintegration, treat comorbidities. Consider digital detox. Prognosis: variable; responds to early intervention.

Core Clinical Content

ICD-11 gaming disorder

Persistent gaming >=12 months; significant impairment in functioning

Prevalence

1-3% adolescents; M:F 3:1; peak 12-18 yrs

Diagnostic features

Impaired control, increasing priority, continuation despite harm

Comorbidity

ADHD, anxiety, depression, ASD, OCD features

Risk factors

Male, low school engagement, social isolation, parental gaming

Functional impact

Sleep, school, family conflict, mood, social withdrawal

First-line

CBT (family-based), motivational interviewing, screen-time contracts

Pharmacotherapy

For comorbidities only (e.g., stimulants for ADHD)

Digital detox

Structured screen-free periods; environmental modification

Prognosis

Variable; better with early intervention, family support

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ICD-11 gaming disorderPersistent gaming >=12 months; significant impairment in functioning
Prevalence1-3% adolescents; M:F 3:1; peak 12-18 yrs
Diagnostic featuresImpaired control, increasing priority, continuation despite harm
ComorbidityADHD, anxiety, depression, ASD, OCD features
Risk factorsMale, low school engagement, social isolation, parental gaming
Functional impactSleep, school, family conflict, mood, social withdrawal
First-lineCBT (family-based), motivational interviewing, screen-time contracts
PharmacotherapyFor comorbidities only (e.g., stimulants for ADHD)
Digital detoxStructured screen-free periods; environmental modification
PrognosisVariable; better with early intervention, family support

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Gaming disorder and internet addiction.

Gaming disorder (ICD-11, 2018) is characterised by a pattern of persistent or recurrent gaming behaviour (digital or video gaming) of sufficient severity to result in significant impairment in personal, family, social, educational, occupational or other important areas of functioning, typically evident over at least 12 months. Internet addiction (broader concept) is not in ICD-11 but includes compulsive internet use affecting school, social and family life. Prevalence: 1-3% adolescents; M:F 3:1; peak 12-18 yrs. Often comorbid with ADHD, anxiety, depression, ASD. Management: CBT (often family-based), motivational interviewing, screen-time contracts, school reintegration, treat comorbidities. Consider digital detox. Prognosis: variable; responds to early intervention.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Gaming disorder and internet addiction?

ICD-11 gaming disorder: Persistent gaming >=12 months; significant impairment in functioning. Prevalence: 1-3% adolescents; M:F 3:1; peak 12-18 yrs. Diagnostic features: Impaired control, increasing priority, continuation despite harm. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Gaming disorder and internet addiction recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Comorbidity - ADHD, anxiety, depression, ASD, OCD features; Risk factors - Male, low school engagement, social isolation, parental gaming. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Gaming disorder and internet addiction?

Investigations include: Functional impact - Sleep, school, family conflict, mood, social withdrawal; First-line - CBT (family-based), motivational interviewing, screen-time contracts. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Gaming disorder and internet addiction?

First-line management: Pharmacotherapy - For comorbidities only (e.g., stimulants for ADHD); Digital detox - Structured screen-free periods; environmental modification. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Gaming disorder and internet addiction?

Red flags and complications: Prognosis - Variable; better with early intervention, family support. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gaming disorder and internet addiction - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

▸ 31.5.6 Ocd In Adolescents

Definition

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) in adolescents is characterised by obsessions (intrusive, unwanted thoughts causing anxiety) and/or compulsions (repetitive behaviours performed to reduce anxiety) that are time-consuming (>1 hr/day) or cause significant distress/impairment. Prevalence is 1-2% in adolescents; M=F; often comorbid with tics, ADHD, anxiety, depression. Onset typically 8-12 yrs. Diagnosis uses DSM-5 criteria adapted for developmental level. Management is multimodal: CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP) first-line, SSRI (fluoxetine/fluvoxamine, higher doses than depression) as pharmacological adjunct. Family accommodation worsens prognosis. Prognosis: chronic waxing-waning course; ~40% recover.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

1-2% adolescents; M=F; onset 8-12 yrs

DSM-5 criteria

Obsessions and/or compulsions; >1 hr/day; distress/impairment

Obsessions

Intrusive unwanted thoughts (contamination, harm, symmetry, sexual/religious)

Compulsions

Repetitive behaviours (washing, checking, counting, ordering)

Comorbidity

Tics (30%), ADHD, anxiety, depression, eating disorders, ASD

Differential

OCD personality, hoarding (now separate), body dysmorphic, autism rituals

First-line therapy

CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP)

SSRI

Fluoxetine, fluvoxamine; doses 2-3x depression; 8-12 weeks trial

Clomipramine

TCA; second-line; ECG required (QT prolongation)

Family accommodation

Reducing family accommodation improves outcomes

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence1-2% adolescents; M=F; onset 8-12 yrs
DSM-5 criteriaObsessions and/or compulsions; >1 hr/day; distress/impairment
ObsessionsIntrusive unwanted thoughts (contamination, harm, symmetry, sexual/religious)
CompulsionsRepetitive behaviours (washing, checking, counting, ordering)
ComorbidityTics (30%), ADHD, anxiety, depression, eating disorders, ASD
DifferentialOCD personality, hoarding (now separate), body dysmorphic, autism rituals
First-line therapyCBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP)
SSRIFluoxetine, fluvoxamine; doses 2-3x depression; 8-12 weeks trial
ClomipramineTCA; second-line; ECG required (QT prolongation)
Family accommodationReducing family accommodation improves outcomes

Viva Questions

Q1. Define OCD in adolescents.

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) in adolescents is characterised by obsessions (intrusive, unwanted thoughts causing anxiety) and/or compulsions (repetitive behaviours performed to reduce anxiety) that are time-consuming (>1 hr/day) or cause significant distress/impairment. Prevalence is 1-2% in adolescents; M=F; often comorbid with tics, ADHD, anxiety, depression. Onset typically 8-12 yrs. Diagnosis uses DSM-5 criteria adapted for developmental level. Management is multimodal: CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP) first-line, SSRI (fluoxetine/fluvoxamine, higher doses than depression) as pharmacological adjunct. Family accommodation worsens prognosis. Prognosis: chronic waxing-waning course; ~40% recover.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for OCD in adolescents?

Prevalence: 1-2% adolescents; M=F; onset 8-12 yrs. DSM-5 criteria: Obsessions and/or compulsions; >1 hr/day; distress/impairment. Obsessions: Intrusive unwanted thoughts (contamination, harm, symmetry, sexual/religious). See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is OCD in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Compulsions - Repetitive behaviours (washing, checking, counting, ordering); Comorbidity - Tics (30%), ADHD, anxiety, depression, eating disorders, ASD. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for OCD in adolescents?

Investigations include: Differential - OCD personality, hoarding (now separate), body dysmorphic, autism rituals; First-line therapy - CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of OCD in adolescents?

First-line management: SSRI - Fluoxetine, fluvoxamine; doses 2-3x depression; 8-12 weeks trial; Clomipramine - TCA; second-line; ECG required (QT prolongation). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of OCD in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Family accommodation - Reducing family accommodation improves outcomes. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for OCD in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

C) Repetitive behaviours (washing, checking, counting, ordering)

D) Tics (30%), ADHD, anxiety, depression, eating disorders, ASD

Answer: B

C) CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP)

D) Repetitive behaviours (washing, checking, counting, ordering)

Answer: D

A) TCA; second-line; ECG required (QT prolongation)

B) Reducing family accommodation improves outcomes

C) 1-2% adolescents; M=F; onset 8-12 yrs

D) Fluoxetine, fluvoxamine; doses 2-3x depression; 8-12 weeks trial

Answer: D

A) 1-2% adolescents; M=F; onset 8-12 yrs

B) Reducing family accommodation improves outcomes

C) Obsessions and/or compulsions; >1 hr/day; distress/impairment

D) Intrusive unwanted thoughts (contamination, harm, symmetry, sexual/religious)

Answer: B

▸ 31.5.7 Ptsd In Adolescents

Definition

Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in adolescents follows exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence (directly, witnessed, or indirectly via family). Symptoms cluster in 4 groups: intrusion (flashbacks, nightmares), avoidance (of trauma reminders), negative alterations in cognition/mood (guilt, shame, dissociation), and hyperarousal (sleep, concentration, anger). Onset may be acute (<3 months) or delayed (>6 months). Prevalence 1-5% in adolescents; common after ACEs, abuse, accidents, war. Management: trauma-focused CBT (TF-CBT), EMDR for older adolescents, SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine); avoid debriefing immediately post-trauma. Family involvement essential. Prognosis: 50% recover within 1 year with treatment.

Core Clinical Content

Trauma criteria

Actual/threatened death, serious injury, sexual violence (direct, witnessed, family)

DSM-5 symptom clusters

Intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, hyperarousal

Duration

1 month; acute <3 months, chronic >3 months, delayed >6 months onset

Prevalence

1-5% adolescents; F > M after puberty

Common triggers

ACEs, abuse, accidents, war/refugee, severe illness, bullying

First-line therapy

Trauma-focused CBT (TF-CBT) gold standard

EMDR

Eye movement desensitisation reprocessing; effective for older adolescents

SSRI

Sertraline, fluoxetine; licensed for PTSD in adults; off-label adolescent

Avoid

Single-session debriefing (no evidence); benzodiazepines (worsen outcomes)

Family role

Non-blame, support, avoid reminders, normalise reactions

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Trauma criteriaActual/threatened death, serious injury, sexual violence (direct, witnessed, family)
DSM-5 symptom clustersIntrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, hyperarousal
Duration>1 month; acute <3 months, chronic >3 months, delayed >6 months onset
Prevalence1-5% adolescents; F > M after puberty
Common triggersACEs, abuse, accidents, war/refugee, severe illness, bullying
First-line therapyTrauma-focused CBT (TF-CBT) gold standard
EMDREye movement desensitisation reprocessing; effective for older adolescents
SSRISertraline, fluoxetine; licensed for PTSD in adults; off-label adolescent
AvoidSingle-session debriefing (no evidence); benzodiazepines (worsen outcomes)
Family roleNon-blame, support, avoid reminders, normalise reactions

Viva Questions

Q1. Define PTSD in adolescents.

Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in adolescents follows exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence (directly, witnessed, or indirectly via family). Symptoms cluster in 4 groups: intrusion (flashbacks, nightmares), avoidance (of trauma reminders), negative alterations in cognition/mood (guilt, shame, dissociation), and hyperarousal (sleep, concentration, anger). Onset may be acute (<3 months) or delayed (>6 months). Prevalence 1-5% in adolescents; common after ACEs, abuse, accidents, war. Management: trauma-focused CBT (TF-CBT), EMDR for older adolescents, SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine); avoid debriefing immediately post-trauma. Family involvement essential. Prognosis: 50% recover within 1 year with treatment.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for PTSD in adolescents?

Trauma criteria: Actual/threatened death, serious injury, sexual violence (direct, witnessed, family). DSM-5 symptom clusters: Intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, hyperarousal. Duration: >1 month; acute <3 months, chronic >3 months, delayed >6 months onset. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is PTSD in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Prevalence - 1-5% adolescents; F > M after puberty; Common triggers - ACEs, abuse, accidents, war/refugee, severe illness, bullying. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for PTSD in adolescents?

Investigations include: First-line therapy - Trauma-focused CBT (TF-CBT) gold standard; EMDR - Eye movement desensitisation reprocessing; effective for older adolescents. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of PTSD in adolescents?

First-line management: SSRI - Sertraline, fluoxetine; licensed for PTSD in adults; off-label adolescent; Avoid - Single-session debriefing (no evidence); benzodiazepines (worsen outcomes). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of PTSD in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Family role - Non-blame, support, avoid reminders, normalise reactions. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for PTSD in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

A) Eye movement desensitisation reprocessing; effective for old B) Sertraline, fluoxetine; licensed for PTSD in adults; off-lab C) Single-session debriefing (no evidence); benzodiazepines (wo D) Trauma-focused CBT (TF-CBT) gold standard Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with PTSD in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding EMDR? A) Sertraline, fluoxetine; licensed for PTSD in adults; off-lab B) Single-session debriefing (no evidence); benzodiazepines (wo C) Non-blame, support, avoid reminders, normalise reactions D) Eye movement desensitisation reprocessing; effective for old Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with PTSD in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding SSRI? A) Single-session debriefing (no evidence); benzodiazepines (wo B) Non-blame, support, avoid reminders, normalise reactions C) Actual/threatened death, serious injury, sexual violence (di D) Sertraline, fluoxetine; licensed for PTSD in adults; off-lab Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with PTSD in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Avoid? A) Non-blame, support, avoid reminders, normalise reactions B) Actual/threatened death, serious injury, sexual violence (di C) Intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, hyperarousal D) Single-session debriefing (no evidence); benzodiazepines (wo Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with PTSD in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Family role? A) Actual/threatened death, serious injury, sexual violence (di B) Intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, hyperarousal C) >1 month; acute <3 months, chronic >3 months, delayed >6 mon D) Non-blame, support, avoid reminders, normalise reactions Answer: D

Flashcards

  • Q: Trauma criteria?

A: Actual/threatened death, serious injury, sexual violence (direct, witnessed, family)

  • Q: DSM-5 symptom clusters?

A: Intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, hyperarousal

  • Q: Duration?

A: >1 month; acute <3 months, chronic >3 months, delayed >6 months onset

  • Q: Prevalence?

A: 1-5% adolescents; F > M after puberty

  • Q: Common triggers?

A: ACEs, abuse, accidents, war/refugee, severe illness, bullying

  • Q: First-line therapy?

A: Trauma-focused CBT (TF-CBT) gold standard

  • Q: EMDR?

A: Eye movement desensitisation reprocessing; effective for older adolescents

  • Q: SSRI?

A: Sertraline, fluoxetine; licensed for PTSD in adults; off-label adolescent

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 31.5.8 Tic Disorders And Tourette Syndrome

Definition

Tic disorders are neurodevelopmental conditions characterised by sudden, rapid, recurrent, non-rhythmic motor movements or vocalisations. Provisional tic disorder (<1 year), persistent (chronic) motor or vocal tic disorder (>1 year, one type), and Tourette syndrome (>1 year, multiple motor + at least one vocal tic) are the main DSM-5 categories. Onset before age 18. Prevalence: Tourette 1%, chronic motor tic 1-2%, provisional tic 5-20% (transient in childhood). M:F 3:1. Strong comorbidity with ADHD (50%), OCD (30-50%), anxiety, learning disability. Management: reassurance for mild; behavioural therapy (CBIT — Comprehensive Behavioral Intervention for Tics); alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, guanfacine) for moderate; antipsychotics (risperidone, aripiprazole) for severe refractory. Most tic disorders improve in adolescence/adulthood.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Sudden rapid recurrent non-rhythmic movements (motor) or vocalisations (vocal)

Provisional tic disorder

Duration <1 year (often transient in childhood)

Persistent tic disorder

1 year; single type (motor OR vocal)

Tourette syndrome

1 year; multiple motor + >=1 vocal tic

Prevalence

Tourette 1%; chronic motor tic 1-2%; provisional 5-20%

Onset

Mean 6-7 yrs; usually pre-adolescence; tics peak 10-12 yrs

Comorbidity

ADHD (50%), OCD (30-50%), anxiety, learning disability

First-line

Reassurance for mild; CBIT (behavioural therapy) for moderate

Pharmacotherapy

Clonidine, guanfacine; risperidone, aripiprazole for severe

Prognosis

Most improve by late adolescence/adulthood; 1/3 persist

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionSudden rapid recurrent non-rhythmic movements (motor) or vocalisations (vocal)
Provisional tic disorderDuration <1 year (often transient in childhood)
Persistent tic disorder>1 year; single type (motor OR vocal)
Tourette syndrome>1 year; multiple motor + >=1 vocal tic
PrevalenceTourette 1%; chronic motor tic 1-2%; provisional 5-20%
OnsetMean 6-7 yrs; usually pre-adolescence; tics peak 10-12 yrs
ComorbidityADHD (50%), OCD (30-50%), anxiety, learning disability
First-lineReassurance for mild; CBIT (behavioural therapy) for moderate
PharmacotherapyClonidine, guanfacine; risperidone, aripiprazole for severe
PrognosisMost improve by late adolescence/adulthood; 1/3 persist

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Tic disorders and Tourette syndrome.

Tic disorders are neurodevelopmental conditions characterised by sudden, rapid, recurrent, non-rhythmic motor movements or vocalisations. Provisional tic disorder (<1 year), persistent (chronic) motor or vocal tic disorder (>1 year, one type), and Tourette syndrome (>1 year, multiple motor + at least one vocal tic) are the main DSM-5 categories. Onset before age 18. Prevalence: Tourette 1%, chronic motor tic 1-2%, provisional tic 5-20% (transient in childhood). M:F 3:1. Strong comorbidity with ADHD (50%), OCD (30-50%), anxiety, learning disability. Management: reassurance for mild; behavioural therapy (CBIT — Comprehensive Behavioral Intervention for Tics); alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, guanfacine) for moderate; antipsychotics (risperidone, aripiprazole) for severe refractory. Most tic disorders improve in adolescence/adulthood.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Tic disorders and Tourette syndrome?

Definition: Sudden rapid recurrent non-rhythmic movements (motor) or vocalisations (vocal). Provisional tic disorder: Duration <1 year (often transient in childhood). Persistent tic disorder: >1 year; single type (motor OR vocal). See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Tic disorders and Tourette syndrome recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Tourette syndrome - >1 year; multiple motor + >=1 vocal tic; Prevalence - Tourette 1%; chronic motor tic 1-2%; provisional 5-20%. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Tic disorders and Tourette syndrome?

Investigations include: Onset - Mean 6-7 yrs; usually pre-adolescence; tics peak 10-12 yrs; Comorbidity - ADHD (50%), OCD (30-50%), anxiety, learning disability. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Tic disorders and Tourette syndrome?

First-line management: First-line - Reassurance for mild; CBIT (behavioural therapy) for moderate; Pharmacotherapy - Clonidine, guanfacine; risperidone, aripiprazole for severe. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Tic disorders and Tourette syndrome?

Red flags and complications: Prognosis - Most improve by late adolescence/adulthood; 1/3 persist. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Tic disorders and Tourette syndrome - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Definition in Tic disorders and Tourette syndrome?

Flashcards

  • Q: Definition?

  • Q: Comorbidity?

A: ADHD (50%), OCD (30-50%), anxiety, learning disability

  • Q: First-line?

A: Reassurance for mild; CBIT (behavioural therapy) for moderate

Answer Key with Explanations

  9. A - Pharmacotherapy: Clonidine, guanfacine; risperidone, aripiprazole for severe
  10. B - Prognosis: Most improve by late adolescence/adulthood; 1/3 persist

  6. D - Onset: Mean 6-7 yrs; usually pre-adolescence; tics peak 10-12 yrs
  7. D - Comorbidity: ADHD (50%), OCD (30-50%), anxiety, learning disability
  8. D - First-line: Reassurance for mild; CBIT (behavioural therapy) for moderat
  9. D - Pharmacotherapy: Clonidine, guanfacine; risperidone, aripiprazole for severe
  10. D - Prognosis: Most improve by late adolescence/adulthood; 1/3 persist

▸ 31.6 Adolescent Musculoskeletal and Sports 2
▸ 31.6.1 Back Pain And Scoliosis In Adolescents

Definition

Back pain affects 20-30% of adolescents; most is mechanical/postural, but serious causes (infection, tumour, scoliosis, spondylolysis, spondyloarthropathy) must be excluded. Red flags: night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss. Idiopathic scoliosis (most common, 2-3% of adolescents, F:M 4:1) presents in adolescence; Cobb angle measurement on standing X-ray guides management (observation <25 degrees, bracing 25-45 degrees, surgery >45-50 degrees). Spondylolysis (pars interarticularis defect) is common in hyperextension athletes (gymnasts, fast bowlers).

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence back pain

20-30% of adolescents

Most common

Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting

Red flags

Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss

Specific causes

Spondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour, spondyloarthropathy

Idiopathic scoliosis

2-3% of adolescents; F:M 4:1; Cobb angle measurement

Scoliosis management

Observation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature); surgery >45-50 deg

Spondylolysis

Pars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling); SPECT/MRI

Scheuermann's disease

Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis

Adams forward bend test

Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side

When to image

Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurological signs

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence back pain20-30% of adolescents
Most commonMechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting
Red flagsNight pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss
Specific causesSpondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour, spondyloarthropathy
Idiopathic scoliosis2-3% of adolescents; F:M 4:1; Cobb angle measurement
Scoliosis managementObservation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature); surgery >45-50 deg
SpondylolysisPars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling); SPECT/MRI
Scheuermann's diseaseVertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis
Adams forward bend testScreening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side
When to imageRed flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurological signs

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents.

Back pain affects 20-30% of adolescents; most is mechanical/postural, but serious causes (infection, tumour, scoliosis, spondylolysis, spondyloarthropathy) must be excluded. Red flags: night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss. Idiopathic scoliosis (most common, 2-3% of adolescents, F:M 4:1) presents in adolescence; Cobb angle measurement on standing X-ray guides management (observation <25 degrees, bracing 25-45 degrees, surgery >45-50 degrees). Spondylolysis (pars interarticularis defect) is common in hyperextension athletes (gymnasts, fast bowlers).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

Prevalence back pain: 20-30% of adolescents. Most common: Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting. Red flags: Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Specific causes - Spondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour, spondyloarthropathy; Idiopathic scoliosis - 2-3% of adolescents; F:M 4:1; Cobb angle measurement. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

Investigations include: Scoliosis management - Observation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature); surgery >45-50 deg; Spondylolysis - Pars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling); SPECT/MRI. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

First-line management: Scheuermann's disease - Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis; Adams forward bend test - Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: When to image - Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurological signs. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

3. AcuteStabilise; treat emergencies; safeguarding
4. Long-termMultidisciplinary team; transition planning
5. MonitorMental health, adherence, growth, puberty

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Prevalence back pain in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) 20-30% of adolescents

B) Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting

C) Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss

D) Spondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour, spondyloarthropathy

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Most common in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss

B) Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting

C) Spondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour, spondyloarthropathy

D) 2-3% of adolescents; F:M 4:1; Cobb angle measurement

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Red flags in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) Spondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour, spondyloarthropathy

B) 2-3% of adolescents; F:M 4:1; Cobb angle measurement

C) Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss

D) Observation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature); surgery >45-50 deg

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Specific causes in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) 2-3% of adolescents; F:M 4:1; Cobb angle measurement

B) Observation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature); surgery >45-50 deg

C) Pars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling); SPECT/MRI

D) Spondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour, spondyloarthropathy

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Idiopathic scoliosis in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) 2-3% of adolescents; F:M 4:1; Cobb angle measurement

B) Observation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature); surgery >45-50 deg

C) Pars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling); SPECT/MRI

D) Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Scoliosis management in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) Pars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling); SPECT/MRI

B) Observation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature); surgery >45-50 deg

C) Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis

D) Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Spondylolysis in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis

B) Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side

C) Pars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling); SPECT/MRI

D) Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurological signs

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Scheuermann's disease in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side

B) Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurological signs

C) 20-30% of adolescents

D) Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Adams forward bend test in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side

B) Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurological signs

C) 20-30% of adolescents

D) Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding When to image in Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents?

A) 20-30% of adolescents

B) Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurological signs

C) Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting

D) Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss

Answer: B

  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Spondylolysis?   1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Scheuermann's disease? A) Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side B) Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurologic C) 20-30% of adolescents D) Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Adams forward bend test? A) Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurologic B) 20-30% of adolescents C) Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting D) Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Back pain and scoliosis in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding When to image? A) 20-30% of adolescents B) Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting C) Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10 D) Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurologic Answer: D

Flashcards

  • Q: Prevalence back pain?

A: 20-30% of adolescents

  • Q: Most common?

A: Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting

  • Q: Red flags?

A: Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10, weight loss

  • Q: Specific causes?

A: Spondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour, spondyloarthropathy

  • Q: Idiopathic scoliosis?

A: 2-3% of adolescents; F:M 4:1; Cobb angle measurement

  • Q: Scoliosis management?

A: Observation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature); surgery >45-50 deg

  • Q: Spondylolysis?

A: Pars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling); SPECT/MRI

  • Q: Scheuermann's disease?

A: Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A - Prevalence back pain: 20-30% of adolescents
  2. B - Most common: Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting
  3. C - Red flags: Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10
  4. D - Specific causes: Spondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour,

  6. B - Scoliosis management: Observation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature)
  7. C - Spondylolysis: Pars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling
  8. D - Scheuermann's disease: Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis
  9. A - Adams forward bend test: Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side
  10. B - When to image: Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurologic

SBAs

  2. D - Most common: Mechanical/postural; benign and self-limiting
  3. D - Red flags: Night pain, systemic symptoms, neurological deficit, age <10
  4. D - Specific causes: Spondylolysis, scoliosis, Scheuermann's, infection, tumour,
  5. D - Idiopathic scoliosis: 2-3% of adolescents; F:M 4:1; Cobb angle measurement
  6. D - Scoliosis management: Observation <25 deg; bracing 25-45 deg (skeletally immature)
  7. D - Spondylolysis: Pars defect; hyperextension sports (gymnastics, fast bowling
  8. D - Scheuermann's disease: Vertebral wedging >5 deg in >=3 vertebrae; thoracic kyphosis
  9. D - Adams forward bend test: Screening for scoliosis; rib hump on convex side
  10. D - When to image: Red flags, scoliosis, persistent pain >4-6 weeks, neurologic
