▸ 31.6.2 Sports Medicine In Adolescents

Definition

Sports medicine in adolescents addresses the unique physiology of the young athlete, including open physes (growth plates), thermoregulation, and psychosocial development. Common conditions: apophysitis (Osgood-Schlatter, Severs), stress fractures, osteochondritis dissecans, concussion, anterior knee pain. Pre-participation evaluation screens for cardiac disease (HOCM, long QT), Marfan, asthma, and eating disorders in athletes. Sudden cardiac death is rare but the leading cause of death in young athletes. Concussion management is conservative with graduated return-to-play protocols (Concussion in Sport Group).

Core Clinical Content

Apophysitis

Traction injury at growth plate; Osgood-Schlatter (tibial tubercle), Severs (calcaneus)

Osgood-Schlatter

Commonest cause of anterior knee pain in adolescents; self-limiting

Stress fractures

Common in running athletes; tibial, metatarsal; low-risk vs high-risk

Osteochondritis dissecans

Subchondral bone necrosis; knee, ankle; surgery if unstable

Anterior knee pain

Most common MSK complaint; usually patellofemoral; physiotherapy

Pre-participation evaluation

Cardiac history/exam; family history; asthma; eating disorder screen

Sudden cardiac death

HOCM, anomalous coronary, long QT, Marfan, commotio cordis

Concussion

Sport-related; SCAT5 assessment; rest 24-48 hr; graduated return-to-play

Female athlete triad

Disordered eating, amenorrhoea, low BMD - RED-S (Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport)

Recovery

Adequate rest, sleep, nutrition; overtraining syndrome - chronic fatigue, mood

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ApophysitisTraction injury at growth plate; Osgood-Schlatter (tibial tubercle), Severs (calcaneus)
Osgood-SchlatterCommonest cause of anterior knee pain in adolescents; self-limiting
Stress fracturesCommon in running athletes; tibial, metatarsal; low-risk vs high-risk
Osteochondritis dissecansSubchondral bone necrosis; knee, ankle; surgery if unstable
Anterior knee painMost common MSK complaint; usually patellofemoral; physiotherapy
Pre-participation evaluationCardiac history/exam; family history; asthma; eating disorder screen
Sudden cardiac deathHOCM, anomalous coronary, long QT, Marfan, commotio cordis
ConcussionSport-related; SCAT5 assessment; rest 24-48 hr; graduated return-to-play
Female athlete triadDisordered eating, amenorrhoea, low BMD - RED-S (Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport)
RecoveryAdequate rest, sleep, nutrition; overtraining syndrome - chronic fatigue, mood

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Sports medicine in adolescents.

Sports medicine in adolescents addresses the unique physiology of the young athlete, including open physes (growth plates), thermoregulation, and psychosocial development. Common conditions: apophysitis (Osgood-Schlatter, Severs), stress fractures, osteochondritis dissecans, concussion, anterior knee pain. Pre-participation evaluation screens for cardiac disease (HOCM, long QT), Marfan, asthma, and eating disorders in athletes. Sudden cardiac death is rare but the leading cause of death in young athletes. Concussion management is conservative with graduated return-to-play protocols (Concussion in Sport Group).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Sports medicine in adolescents?

Apophysitis: Traction injury at growth plate; Osgood-Schlatter (tibial tubercle), Severs (calcaneus). Osgood-Schlatter: Commonest cause of anterior knee pain in adolescents; self-limiting. Stress fractures: Common in running athletes; tibial, metatarsal; low-risk vs high-risk. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Sports medicine in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Osteochondritis dissecans - Subchondral bone necrosis; knee, ankle; surgery if unstable; Anterior knee pain - Most common MSK complaint; usually patellofemoral; physiotherapy. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Sports medicine in adolescents?

Investigations include: Pre-participation evaluation - Cardiac history/exam; family history; asthma; eating disorder screen; Sudden cardiac death - HOCM, anomalous coronary, long QT, Marfan, commotio cordis. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Sports medicine in adolescents?

First-line management: Concussion - Sport-related; SCAT5 assessment; rest 24-48 hr; graduated return-to-play; Female athlete triad - Disordered eating, amenorrhoea, low BMD - RED-S (Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Sports medicine in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Recovery - Adequate rest, sleep, nutrition; overtraining syndrome - chronic fatigue, mood. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sports medicine in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

Sports medicine in adolescents
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Recognition
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Examination
├── Investigation
├── Management
│   ├── Acute
│   └── Long-term
└── Complications
    ├── Short-term
    └── Long-term

StepAction
1. RecognitionIdentify key features
2. AssessmentHEEADSSS psychosocial screen + focused exam
3. AcuteStabilise; treat emergencies; safeguarding
4. Long-termMultidisciplinary team; transition planning
5. MonitorMental health, adherence, growth, puberty

  2. D - Osgood-Schlatter: Commonest cause of anterior knee pain in adolescents; self-l
  3. D - Stress fractures: Common in running athletes; tibial, metatarsal; low-risk vs
  4. D - Osteochondritis dissecans: Subchondral bone necrosis; knee, ankle; surgery if unstable
  5. D - Anterior knee pain: Most common MSK complaint; usually patellofemoral; physiothe
  6. D - Pre-participation evaluation: Cardiac history/exam; family history; asthma; eating disorde
  7. D - Sudden cardiac death: HOCM, anomalous coronary, long QT, Marfan, commotio cordis
  8. D - Concussion: Sport-related; SCAT5 assessment; rest 24-48 hr; graduated re
  9. D - Female athlete triad: Disordered eating, amenorrhoea, low BMD - RED-S (Relative En
  10. D - Recovery: Adequate rest, sleep, nutrition; overtraining syndrome - chr

▸ 31.7 Adolescent Presenting Complaints 7
▸ 31.7.1 Adhd In Adolescents

Definition

Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by persistent inattention, hyperactivity and impulsivity that interferes with function. It affects 5-7% of children/adolescents, F:M 1:2-3, often persisting into adulthood. Diagnosis is clinical (DSM-5/ICD-11) with symptoms present before age 12, in >=2 settings (school, home), and causing impairment. ADHD has high comorbidity with oppositional defiant disorder (ODD), conduct disorder, anxiety, depression, substance use, and learning disability. Management is multimodal: psychoeducation, school accommodations, behavioural therapy, and pharmacotherapy (methylphenidate first-line, lisdexamfetamine, atomoxetine, guanfacine).

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

5-7% of children/adolescents; F:M 1:2-3; persists into adulthood in 50-70%

DSM-5 subtypes

Inattentive, hyperactive-impulsive, combined

DSM-5 criteria

=6 symptoms (>=5 if >=17 yrs); >=6 months; onset before 12; >=2 settings

Inattention symptoms

Careless mistakes, difficulty sustaining attention, distractible, loses things, forgetful

Hyperactivity symptoms

Fidgets, leaves seat, runs/climbs, can't play quietly, on the go, talks excessively

Impulsivity

Blurts out, can't wait turn, interrupts

Comorbidity

ODD (50%), conduct disorder (25%), anxiety (25%), depression, SUD, learning disability

Differential

Anxiety, depression, sleep disorder, learning disability, autism, thyroid

Assessment

Conners, Vanderbilt, ADHD-RS scales; school reports; collateral history

Treatment

Methylphenidate first-line; lisdexamfetamine; atomoxetine (non-stimulant); guanfacine

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence5-7% of children/adolescents; F:M 1:2-3; persists into adulthood in 50-70%
DSM-5 subtypesInattentive, hyperactive-impulsive, combined
DSM-5 criteria>=6 symptoms (>=5 if >=17 yrs); >=6 months; onset before 12; >=2 settings
Inattention symptomsCareless mistakes, difficulty sustaining attention, distractible, loses things, forgetful
Hyperactivity symptomsFidgets, leaves seat, runs/climbs, can't play quietly, on the go, talks excessively
ImpulsivityBlurts out, can't wait turn, interrupts
ComorbidityODD (50%), conduct disorder (25%), anxiety (25%), depression, SUD, learning disability
DifferentialAnxiety, depression, sleep disorder, learning disability, autism, thyroid
AssessmentConners, Vanderbilt, ADHD-RS scales; school reports; collateral history
TreatmentMethylphenidate first-line; lisdexamfetamine; atomoxetine (non-stimulant); guanfacine

Viva Questions

Q1. Define ADHD in adolescents.

Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by persistent inattention, hyperactivity and impulsivity that interferes with function. It affects 5-7% of children/adolescents, F:M 1:2-3, often persisting into adulthood. Diagnosis is clinical (DSM-5/ICD-11) with symptoms present before age 12, in >=2 settings (school, home), and causing impairment. ADHD has high comorbidity with oppositional defiant disorder (ODD), conduct disorder, anxiety, depression, substance use, and learning disability. Management is multimodal: psychoeducation, school accommodations, behavioural therapy, and pharmacotherapy (methylphenidate first-line, lisdexamfetamine, atomoxetine, guanfacine).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for ADHD in adolescents?

Prevalence: 5-7% of children/adolescents; F:M 1:2-3; persists into adulthood in 50-70%. DSM-5 subtypes: Inattentive, hyperactive-impulsive, combined. DSM-5 criteria: >=6 symptoms (>=5 if >=17 yrs); >=6 months; onset before 12; >=2 settings. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is ADHD in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Inattention symptoms - Careless mistakes, difficulty sustaining attention, distractible, loses things, forgetful; Hyperactivity symptoms - Fidgets, leaves seat, runs/climbs, can't play quietly, on the go, talks excessively. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for ADHD in adolescents?

Investigations include: Impulsivity - Blurts out, can't wait turn, interrupts; Comorbidity - ODD (50%), conduct disorder (25%), anxiety (25%), depression, SUD, learning disability. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of ADHD in adolescents?

First-line management: Differential - Anxiety, depression, sleep disorder, learning disability, autism, thyroid; Assessment - Conners, Vanderbilt, ADHD-RS scales; school reports; collateral history. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of ADHD in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Treatment - Methylphenidate first-line; lisdexamfetamine; atomoxetine (non-stimulant); guanfacine. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for ADHD in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

▸ 31.7.2 Chronic Fatigue Syndrome And Me In Adolescents

Definition

Chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS), also known as myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME), is a complex, multi-system disorder characterised by profound, persistent fatigue lasting >3 months, post-exertional malaise (PEM), unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction ('brain fog'), and often orthostatic intolerance. CFS/ME commonly begins in adolescence (peak onset 11-19 yrs; F:M 3:1) and is a major cause of school absence and disability. Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2021, CDC 2017, IOM 2015 criteria) after exclusion of alternative causes. Management is multi-component: pacing/activity management, sleep hygiene, symptom control, and multidisciplinary team input. Prognosis is variable - ~50% improve over time, but full recovery is uncommon.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Fatigue >3 months + PEM + unrefreshing sleep + cognitive dysfunction

Prevalence

0.1-0.5% adolescents; F:M 3:1; peak onset 11-19 yrs

Diagnostic criteria (NICE 2021)

All 4: fatigue, PEM, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction

Post-exertional malaise (PEM)

Worsening of symptoms 24-72 hr after physical/mental exertion; hallmark

Orthostatic intolerance

POTS-like symptoms; standing test positive in ~50%

Differential

Anaemia, thyroid, coeliac, IBD, depression, anxiety, sleep apnoea, malignancy

Investigations

FBC, ferritin, TFTs, coeliac serology, B12, vitamin D, U&E, LFTs, ESR, urinalysis

Red flag exclusions

Weight loss, lymphadenopathy, fever, night sweats - exclude malignancy/autoimmune

Management

Pacing (energy envelope), sleep hygiene, graded activity, CBT for coping

Prognosis

~50% improve over 5 yrs; full recovery uncommon; early intervention improves outcomes

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionFatigue >3 months + PEM + unrefreshing sleep + cognitive dysfunction
Prevalence0.1-0.5% adolescents; F:M 3:1; peak onset 11-19 yrs
Diagnostic criteria (NICE 2021)All 4: fatigue, PEM, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction
Post-exertional malaise (PEM)Worsening of symptoms 24-72 hr after physical/mental exertion; hallmark
Orthostatic intolerancePOTS-like symptoms; standing test positive in ~50%
DifferentialAnaemia, thyroid, coeliac, IBD, depression, anxiety, sleep apnoea, malignancy
InvestigationsFBC, ferritin, TFTs, coeliac serology, B12, vitamin D, U&E, LFTs, ESR, urinalysis
Red flag exclusionsWeight loss, lymphadenopathy, fever, night sweats - exclude malignancy/autoimmune
ManagementPacing (energy envelope), sleep hygiene, graded activity, CBT for coping
Prognosis~50% improve over 5 yrs; full recovery uncommon; early intervention improves outcomes

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents.

Chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS), also known as myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME), is a complex, multi-system disorder characterised by profound, persistent fatigue lasting >3 months, post-exertional malaise (PEM), unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction ('brain fog'), and often orthostatic intolerance. CFS/ME commonly begins in adolescence (peak onset 11-19 yrs; F:M 3:1) and is a major cause of school absence and disability. Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2021, CDC 2017, IOM 2015 criteria) after exclusion of alternative causes. Management is multi-component: pacing/activity management, sleep hygiene, symptom control, and multidisciplinary team input. Prognosis is variable - ~50% improve over time, but full recovery is uncommon.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

Definition: Fatigue >3 months + PEM + unrefreshing sleep + cognitive dysfunction. Prevalence: 0.1-0.5% adolescents; F:M 3:1; peak onset 11-19 yrs. Diagnostic criteria (NICE 2021): All 4: fatigue, PEM, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Post-exertional malaise (PEM) - Worsening of symptoms 24-72 hr after physical/mental exertion; hallmark; Orthostatic intolerance - POTS-like symptoms; standing test positive in ~50%. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

Investigations include: Differential - Anaemia, thyroid, coeliac, IBD, depression, anxiety, sleep apnoea, malignancy; Investigations - FBC, ferritin, TFTs, coeliac serology, B12, vitamin D, U&E, LFTs, ESR, urinalysis. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

First-line management: Red flag exclusions - Weight loss, lymphadenopathy, fever, night sweats - exclude malignancy/autoimmune; Management - Pacing (energy envelope), sleep hygiene, graded activity, CBT for coping. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Prognosis - ~50% improve over 5 yrs; full recovery uncommon; early intervention improves outcomes. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Post-exertional malaise (PEM) in Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Orthostatic intolerance in Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

A) POTS-like symptoms; standing test positive in ~50%

B) Anaemia, thyroid, coeliac, IBD, depression, anxiety, sleep apnoea, malignancy

C) FBC, ferritin, TFTs, coeliac serology, B12, vitamin D, U&E, LFTs, ESR, urinalysi

D) Weight loss, lymphadenopathy, fever, night sweats - exclude malignancy/autoimmun

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Differential in Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

A) FBC, ferritin, TFTs, coeliac serology, B12, vitamin D, U&E, LFTs, ESR, urinalysi

B) Anaemia, thyroid, coeliac, IBD, depression, anxiety, sleep apnoea, malignancy

C) Weight loss, lymphadenopathy, fever, night sweats - exclude malignancy/autoimmun

D) Pacing (energy envelope), sleep hygiene, graded activity, CBT for coping

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Investigations in Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

A) Weight loss, lymphadenopathy, fever, night sweats - exclude malignancy/autoimmun

B) Pacing (energy envelope), sleep hygiene, graded activity, CBT for coping

C) FBC, ferritin, TFTs, coeliac serology, B12, vitamin D, U&E, LFTs, ESR, urinalysis

D) ~50% improve over 5 yrs; full recovery uncommon; early intervention improves out

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Red flag exclusions in Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

A) Pacing (energy envelope), sleep hygiene, graded activity, CBT for coping

B) ~50% improve over 5 yrs; full recovery uncommon; early intervention improves out

C) Fatigue >3 months + PEM + unrefreshing sleep + cognitive dysfunction

D) Weight loss, lymphadenopathy, fever, night sweats - exclude malignancy/autoimmune

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Management in Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

A) Pacing (energy envelope), sleep hygiene, graded activity, CBT for coping

B) ~50% improve over 5 yrs; full recovery uncommon; early intervention improves out

C) Fatigue >3 months + PEM + unrefreshing sleep + cognitive dysfunction

D) 0.1-0.5% adolescents; F:M 3:1; peak onset 11-19 yrs

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Prognosis in Chronic fatigue syndrome and ME in adolescents?

A) Fatigue >3 months + PEM + unrefreshing sleep + cognitive dysfunction

B) ~50% improve over 5 yrs; full recovery uncommon; early intervention improves outcomes

C) 0.1-0.5% adolescents; F:M 3:1; peak onset 11-19 yrs

D) All 4: fatigue, PEM, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction

Answer: B

Flashcards

  • Q: Definition?

A: Fatigue >3 months + PEM + unrefreshing sleep + cognitive dysfunction

  • Q: Prevalence?

A: 0.1-0.5% adolescents; F:M 3:1; peak onset 11-19 yrs

  • Q: Diagnostic criteria (NICE 2021)?

A: All 4: fatigue, PEM, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction

  • Q: Post-exertional malaise (PEM)?

A: Worsening of symptoms 24-72 hr after physical/mental exertion; hallmark

A: POTS-like symptoms; standing test positive in ~50%

A: Anaemia, thyroid, coeliac, IBD, depression, anxiety, sleep apnoea, malignancy

A: FBC, ferritin, TFTs, coeliac serology, B12, vitamin D, U&E, LFTs, ESR, urinalysis

  • Q: Red flag exclusions?

A: Weight loss, lymphadenopathy, fever, night sweats - exclude malignancy/autoimmune

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 31.7.3 Headache In Adolescents

Definition

Headache is a common adolescent presentation; 50-75% of 12-17 year-olds report recurrent headache. Primary headaches (tension, migraine, cluster) are most common; secondary causes (raised ICP, infection, vascular) are rare but must be excluded. Migraine affects 5-10% of adolescents, F > M, often familial. Tension-type headache is the most common cause of recurrent headache. Red flags: sudden ('thunderclap'), worst-ever, focal neuro deficit, fever, neck stiffness, papilloedema, weight loss, vomiting on waking - all need urgent investigation (CT/MRI, LP if indicated).

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

50-75% adolescents report recurrent headache; 5-10% migraine

Primary types

Tension (most common), migraine, cluster, medication-overuse

Migraine criteria

=5 attacks; 4-72 hr; >=2 of unilateral, throbbing, mod-severe, aggravated by activity

Migraine associated symptoms

Nausea, photophobia, phonophobia, aura (visual/sensory)

Tension headache

Bilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, no nausea, no photophobia

Cluster headache

Severe unilateral periorbital, autonomic features, 15-180 min, clusters

Red flags (urgent imaging)

Thunderclap, worst-ever, focal deficit, fever, papilloedema, weight loss

Triggers

Stress, sleep change, missed meals, dehydration, hormonal, screen, alcohol

Acute migraine Rx

Ibuprofen 400-600 mg or sumatriptan nasal 10-20 mg; early use effective

Prophylaxis

Topiramate, propranolol, amitriptyline, CBT, riboflavin 400 mg

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence50-75% adolescents report recurrent headache; 5-10% migraine
Primary typesTension (most common), migraine, cluster, medication-overuse
Migraine criteria>=5 attacks; 4-72 hr; >=2 of unilateral, throbbing, mod-severe, aggravated by activity
Migraine associated symptomsNausea, photophobia, phonophobia, aura (visual/sensory)
Tension headacheBilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, no nausea, no photophobia
Cluster headacheSevere unilateral periorbital, autonomic features, 15-180 min, clusters
Red flags (urgent imaging)Thunderclap, worst-ever, focal deficit, fever, papilloedema, weight loss
TriggersStress, sleep change, missed meals, dehydration, hormonal, screen, alcohol
Acute migraine RxIbuprofen 400-600 mg or sumatriptan nasal 10-20 mg; early use effective
ProphylaxisTopiramate, propranolol, amitriptyline, CBT, riboflavin 400 mg

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Headache in adolescents.

Headache is a common adolescent presentation; 50-75% of 12-17 year-olds report recurrent headache. Primary headaches (tension, migraine, cluster) are most common; secondary causes (raised ICP, infection, vascular) are rare but must be excluded. Migraine affects 5-10% of adolescents, F > M, often familial. Tension-type headache is the most common cause of recurrent headache. Red flags: sudden ('thunderclap'), worst-ever, focal neuro deficit, fever, neck stiffness, papilloedema, weight loss, vomiting on waking - all need urgent investigation (CT/MRI, LP if indicated).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Headache in adolescents?

Prevalence: 50-75% adolescents report recurrent headache; 5-10% migraine. Primary types: Tension (most common), migraine, cluster, medication-overuse. Migraine criteria: >=5 attacks; 4-72 hr; >=2 of unilateral, throbbing, mod-severe, aggravated by activity. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Headache in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Migraine associated symptoms - Nausea, photophobia, phonophobia, aura (visual/sensory); Tension headache - Bilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, no nausea, no photophobia. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Headache in adolescents?

Investigations include: Cluster headache - Severe unilateral periorbital, autonomic features, 15-180 min, clusters; Red flags (urgent imaging) - Thunderclap, worst-ever, focal deficit, fever, papilloedema, weight loss. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Headache in adolescents?

First-line management: Triggers - Stress, sleep change, missed meals, dehydration, hormonal, screen, alcohol; Acute migraine Rx - Ibuprofen 400-600 mg or sumatriptan nasal 10-20 mg; early use effective. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Headache in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Prophylaxis - Topiramate, propranolol, amitriptyline, CBT, riboflavin 400 mg. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Headache in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

    └── Long-term

StepAction
1. RecognitionIdentify key features
2. AssessmentHEEADSSS psychosocial screen + focused exam
3. AcuteStabilise; treat emergencies; safeguarding
4. Long-termMultidisciplinary team; transition planning
5. MonitorMental health, adherence, growth, puberty

▸ 31.7.4 Medically Unexplained Symptoms And Somatisation In

Definition

Medically unexplained symptoms (MUS) or somatisation are physical complaints without identifiable organic disease, common in adolescents (10-30% of presentations). Presentations include recurrent abdominal pain, headache, fatigue, chest pain, and limb pain. They often reflect distress, anxiety, or psychosocial difficulty. Diagnosis is positive (based on clinical features) rather than by exclusion. Management is multidisciplinary with CBT, family therapy, school engagement, and judicious medical investigation. Red flags: weight loss, night symptoms, abnormal signs. Avoid excessive investigation that reinforces illness behaviour.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

10-30% of adolescent medical presentations; F > M after puberty

Common presentations

Recurrent abdominal pain, headache, chest pain, limb pain, fatigue

Terminology

Somatic symptom disorder (DSM-5), somatoform autonomic dysfunction, functional

Risk factors

Anxiety, depression, family history, recent illness, school stress, ACEs

Maintaining factors

Avoidance, parental anxiety, school absence, secondary gain

Positive diagnosis

Symptoms inconsistent with known physiology; normal exam; no red flags

Red flags

Weight loss, night sweats, abnormal signs, focal neuro deficit, growth failure

Management

Validate symptoms; explain mechanism; CBT; family therapy; school reintegration

Avoid

Repeated normal investigations; 'there is nothing wrong'; dismissive reassurance

Prognosis

60-80% improve with appropriate intervention; risk of chronicity if mismanaged

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence10-30% of adolescent medical presentations; F > M after puberty
Common presentationsRecurrent abdominal pain, headache, chest pain, limb pain, fatigue
TerminologySomatic symptom disorder (DSM-5), somatoform autonomic dysfunction, functional
Risk factorsAnxiety, depression, family history, recent illness, school stress, ACEs
Maintaining factorsAvoidance, parental anxiety, school absence, secondary gain
Positive diagnosisSymptoms inconsistent with known physiology; normal exam; no red flags
Red flagsWeight loss, night sweats, abnormal signs, focal neuro deficit, growth failure
ManagementValidate symptoms; explain mechanism; CBT; family therapy; school reintegration
AvoidRepeated normal investigations; 'there is nothing wrong'; dismissive reassurance
Prognosis60-80% improve with appropriate intervention; risk of chronicity if mismanaged

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Medically unexplained symptoms and somatisation in adolescents.

Medically unexplained symptoms (MUS) or somatisation are physical complaints without identifiable organic disease, common in adolescents (10-30% of presentations). Presentations include recurrent abdominal pain, headache, fatigue, chest pain, and limb pain. They often reflect distress, anxiety, or psychosocial difficulty. Diagnosis is positive (based on clinical features) rather than by exclusion. Management is multidisciplinary with CBT, family therapy, school engagement, and judicious medical investigation. Red flags: weight loss, night symptoms, abnormal signs. Avoid excessive investigation that reinforces illness behaviour.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Medically unexplained symptoms and somatisation in adolescents?

Prevalence: 10-30% of adolescent medical presentations; F > M after puberty. Common presentations: Recurrent abdominal pain, headache, chest pain, limb pain, fatigue. Terminology: Somatic symptom disorder (DSM-5), somatoform autonomic dysfunction, functional. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Medically unexplained symptoms and somatisation in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Risk factors - Anxiety, depression, family history, recent illness, school stress, ACEs; Maintaining factors - Avoidance, parental anxiety, school absence, secondary gain. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Medically unexplained symptoms and somatisation in adolescents?

Investigations include: Positive diagnosis - Symptoms inconsistent with known physiology; normal exam; no red flags; Red flags - Weight loss, night sweats, abnormal signs, focal neuro deficit, growth failure. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Medically unexplained symptoms and somatisation in adolescents?

First-line management: Management - Validate symptoms; explain mechanism; CBT; family therapy; school reintegration; Avoid - Repeated normal investigations; 'there is nothing wrong'; dismissive reassurance. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Medically unexplained symptoms and somatisation in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Prognosis - 60-80% improve with appropriate intervention; risk of chronicity if mismanaged. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Medically unexplained symptoms and somatisation in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

▸ 31.7.5 Scrotal And Penile Disorders In Adolescents

Definition

Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents include acute (testicular torsion - surgical emergency) and chronic (varicocele, hydrocele, epididymal cyst, epididymo-orchitis) scrotal pathology, and penile conditions (phimosis, balanitis, hypospadias sequelae, Peyronie's). Testicular torsion has peak incidence at 12-18 yrs and requires exploration within 6 hrs to save the testis (90% salvage if <6 hr, <10% if >24 hr). Varicocele affects 15% of adolescent males; usually left-sided, 'bag of worms', often asymptomatic but can impair fertility. Cancer awareness: testicular cancer is the commonest solid tumour in 15-35 yr-old males; teach self-examination.

Core Clinical Content

Testicular torsion

Surgical emergency; sudden severe pain, absent cremasteric reflex, high testis

Torsion window

90% salvage <6 hr; <10% salvage >24 hr

Torsion management

Urgent surgical exploration; bilateral orchidopexy

Varicocele

15% adolescent males; left-sided (90%); 'bag of worms'; Valsalva positive

Varicocele management

Observe if small/asymptomatic; surgery if large, painful, or testicular atrophy

Hydrocele

Transilluminates; usually congenital (patent processus); most resolve

Epididymal cyst/spermatocele

Painless, transilluminates, above testis

Epididymo-orchitis

Painful, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn's sign; sexually active - STI screen

Phimosis

Retraction of foreskin impossible; physiological until ~6 yrs; treat if symptomatic

Testicular cancer

Commonest solid tumour 15-35 yr; painless lump; teach self-examination

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Testicular torsionSurgical emergency; sudden severe pain, absent cremasteric reflex, high testis
Torsion window90% salvage <6 hr; <10% salvage >24 hr
Torsion managementUrgent surgical exploration; bilateral orchidopexy
Varicocele15% adolescent males; left-sided (90%); 'bag of worms'; Valsalva positive
Varicocele managementObserve if small/asymptomatic; surgery if large, painful, or testicular atrophy
HydroceleTransilluminates; usually congenital (patent processus); most resolve
Epididymal cyst/spermatocelePainless, transilluminates, above testis
Epididymo-orchitisPainful, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn's sign; sexually active - STI screen
PhimosisRetraction of foreskin impossible; physiological until ~6 yrs; treat if symptomatic
Testicular cancerCommonest solid tumour 15-35 yr; painless lump; teach self-examination

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents.

Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents include acute (testicular torsion - surgical emergency) and chronic (varicocele, hydrocele, epididymal cyst, epididymo-orchitis) scrotal pathology, and penile conditions (phimosis, balanitis, hypospadias sequelae, Peyronie's). Testicular torsion has peak incidence at 12-18 yrs and requires exploration within 6 hrs to save the testis (90% salvage if <6 hr, <10% if >24 hr). Varicocele affects 15% of adolescent males; usually left-sided, 'bag of worms', often asymptomatic but can impair fertility. Cancer awareness: testicular cancer is the commonest solid tumour in 15-35 yr-old males; teach self-examination.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents?

Testicular torsion: Surgical emergency; sudden severe pain, absent cremasteric reflex, high testis. Torsion window: 90% salvage <6 hr; <10% salvage >24 hr. Torsion management: Urgent surgical exploration; bilateral orchidopexy. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Varicocele - 15% adolescent males; left-sided (90%); 'bag of worms'; Valsalva positive; Varicocele management - Observe if small/asymptomatic; surgery if large, painful, or testicular atrophy. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents?

Investigations include: Hydrocele - Transilluminates; usually congenital (patent processus); most resolve; Epididymal cyst/spermatocele - Painless, transilluminates, above testis. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents?

First-line management: Epididymo-orchitis - Painful, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn's sign; sexually active - STI screen; Phimosis - Retraction of foreskin impossible; physiological until ~6 yrs; treat if symptomatic. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Testicular cancer - Commonest solid tumour 15-35 yr; painless lump; teach self-examination. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

A) Observe if small/asymptomatic; surgery if large, painful, or B) Transilluminates; usually congenital (patent processus); mos C) Painless, transilluminates, above testis D) 15% adolescent males; left-sided (90%); 'bag of worms'; Vals Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Varicocele management? A) Transilluminates; usually congenital (patent processus); mos B) Painless, transilluminates, above testis C) Painful, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn's sign; sexually act D) Observe if small/asymptomatic; surgery if large, painful, or Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Hydrocele? A) Painless, transilluminates, above testis B) Painful, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn's sign; sexually act C) Retraction of foreskin impossible; physiological until ~6 yr D) Transilluminates; usually congenital (patent processus); mos Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Epididymal cyst/spermatocele? A) Painful, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn's sign; sexually act B) Retraction of foreskin impossible; physiological until ~6 yr C) Commonest solid tumour 15-35 yr; painless lump; teach self-e D) Painless, transilluminates, above testis Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Epididymo-orchitis? A) Retraction of foreskin impossible; physiological until ~6 yr B) Commonest solid tumour 15-35 yr; painless lump; teach self-e C) Surgical emergency; sudden severe pain, absent cremasteric r D) Painful, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn's sign; sexually act Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Phimosis? A) Commonest solid tumour 15-35 yr; painless lump; teach self-e B) Surgical emergency; sudden severe pain, absent cremasteric r C) 90% salvage <6 hr; <10% salvage >24 hr D) Retraction of foreskin impossible; physiological until ~6 yr Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Scrotal and penile disorders in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Testicular cancer? A) Surgical emergency; sudden severe pain, absent cremasteric r B) 90% salvage <6 hr; <10% salvage >24 hr C) Urgent surgical exploration; bilateral orchidopexy D) Commonest solid tumour 15-35 yr; painless lump; teach self-e Answer: D

Flashcards

  • Q: Testicular torsion?

A: Surgical emergency; sudden severe pain, absent cremasteric reflex, high testis

  • Q: Torsion window?

A: 90% salvage <6 hr; <10% salvage >24 hr

  • Q: Torsion management?

A: Urgent surgical exploration; bilateral orchidopexy

  • Q: Varicocele?

A: 15% adolescent males; left-sided (90%); 'bag of worms'; Valsalva positive

  • Q: Varicocele management?

A: Observe if small/asymptomatic; surgery if large, painful, or testicular atrophy

  • Q: Hydrocele?

A: Transilluminates; usually congenital (patent processus); most resolve

  • Q: Epididymal cyst/spermatocele?

A: Painless, transilluminates, above testis

  • Q: Epididymo-orchitis?

A: Painful, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn's sign; sexually active - STI screen

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  4. D - Varicocele: 15% adolescent males; left-sided (90%); 'bag of worms'; Vals
  5. A - Varicocele management: Observe if small/asymptomatic; surgery if large, painful, or
  6. B - Hydrocele: Transilluminates; usually congenital (patent processus); mos
  7. C - Epididymal cyst/spermatocele: Painless, transilluminates, above testis
  8. D - Epididymo-orchitis: Painful, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn's sign; sexually act
  9. A - Phimosis: Retraction of foreskin impossible; physiological until ~6 yr
  10. B - Testicular cancer: Commonest solid tumour 15-35 yr; painless lump; teach self-e

  6. D - Hydrocele: Transilluminates; usually congenital (patent processus); mos

▸ 31.7.6 Sleep Disorders In Adolescents

Definition

Sleep disorders in adolescents are common and impact physical health, mental health, and academic performance. The two main patterns are: (1) Delayed sleep phase syndrome (DSPS) - circadian rhythm shift common in adolescents, with sleep onset 2-4 am and wake 10 am-2 pm, driven by delayed melatonin release; (2) Insomnia - difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep. Contributing factors: screen time, caffeine, irregular schedules, mental health comorbidity. Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) is less common but important (obesity, craniofacial). Management: sleep hygiene, chronotherapy, melatonin (DSPS), CBT-I (insomnia), CPAP (OSA).

Core Clinical Content

Sleep need

8-10 hrs/night (teens); mean sleep 7-7.5 hrs (under-slept)

Delayed sleep phase syndrome

Circadian shift; sleep onset 2-4 am; common in adolescents

DSPS mechanism

Delayed melatonin release; entrainment to light/dark cycle

Insomnia

Difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep; 10-30% adolescents

Contributing factors

Screen time (blue light), caffeine, irregular schedule, anxiety/depression

Obstructive sleep apnoea

Snoring, daytime sleepiness, obesity, adenotonsillar hypertrophy

Health impact

Mood, cognition, academic performance, metabolic, immune

Sleep hygiene

Regular schedule; no screens 1 hr pre-bed; dark/cool room; avoid caffeine

Treatment DSPS

Chronotherapy, morning bright light, melatonin 0.5-3 mg 4-6 hr pre-bed

Treatment insomnia

CBT-I first-line; sleep restriction; stimulus control

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Sleep need8-10 hrs/night (teens); mean sleep 7-7.5 hrs (under-slept)
Delayed sleep phase syndromeCircadian shift; sleep onset 2-4 am; common in adolescents
DSPS mechanismDelayed melatonin release; entrainment to light/dark cycle
InsomniaDifficulty initiating/maintaining sleep; 10-30% adolescents
Contributing factorsScreen time (blue light), caffeine, irregular schedule, anxiety/depression
Obstructive sleep apnoeaSnoring, daytime sleepiness, obesity, adenotonsillar hypertrophy
Health impactMood, cognition, academic performance, metabolic, immune
Sleep hygieneRegular schedule; no screens 1 hr pre-bed; dark/cool room; avoid caffeine
Treatment DSPSChronotherapy, morning bright light, melatonin 0.5-3 mg 4-6 hr pre-bed
Treatment insomniaCBT-I first-line; sleep restriction; stimulus control

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Sleep disorders in adolescents.

Sleep disorders in adolescents are common and impact physical health, mental health, and academic performance. The two main patterns are: (1) Delayed sleep phase syndrome (DSPS) - circadian rhythm shift common in adolescents, with sleep onset 2-4 am and wake 10 am-2 pm, driven by delayed melatonin release; (2) Insomnia - difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep. Contributing factors: screen time, caffeine, irregular schedules, mental health comorbidity. Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) is less common but important (obesity, craniofacial). Management: sleep hygiene, chronotherapy, melatonin (DSPS), CBT-I (insomnia), CPAP (OSA).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Sleep disorders in adolescents?

Sleep need: 8-10 hrs/night (teens); mean sleep 7-7.5 hrs (under-slept). Delayed sleep phase syndrome: Circadian shift; sleep onset 2-4 am; common in adolescents. DSPS mechanism: Delayed melatonin release; entrainment to light/dark cycle. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Sleep disorders in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Insomnia - Difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep; 10-30% adolescents; Contributing factors - Screen time (blue light), caffeine, irregular schedule, anxiety/depression. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Sleep disorders in adolescents?

Investigations include: Obstructive sleep apnoea - Snoring, daytime sleepiness, obesity, adenotonsillar hypertrophy; Health impact - Mood, cognition, academic performance, metabolic, immune. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Sleep disorders in adolescents?

First-line management: Sleep hygiene - Regular schedule; no screens 1 hr pre-bed; dark/cool room; avoid caffeine; Treatment DSPS - Chronotherapy, morning bright light, melatonin 0.5-3 mg 4-6 hr pre-bed. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Sleep disorders in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Treatment insomnia - CBT-I first-line; sleep restriction; stimulus control. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sleep disorders in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 31.7.7 Vulvar Disorders In Adolescents

Definition

Vulvar disorders in adolescents include vulvovaginitis (most common), labial adhesions, lichen sclerosus, vulvodynia, Bartholin's cyst/abscess, and (rarely) malignancy. Vulvovaginitis is usually non-specific (bacterial) in young adolescents and STI-related (chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas) in sexually active adolescents. Presentation: discharge, pruritus, dysuria, soreness, bleeding. Lichen sclerosus presents with porcelain-white atrophic plaques, often with itching and soreness. Labial adhesions are common in young children, less so in adolescents (consider sexual abuse if severe or sudden). Management: hygiene advice, treat specific infection, topical oestrogen for labial adhesions, potent topical steroid for lichen sclerosus.

Core Clinical Content

Vulvovaginitis causes

Non-specific (prepubertal), Candida (post-pubertal), bacterial, STI

STI-related organisms

Chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas, HSV, HPV

Non-specific vaginitis

Commonest in young adolescents; mixed flora; hygiene-related

Candida vulvovaginitis

Itching, thick white discharge; pH <4.5; treat with clotrimazole/fluconazole

Bacterial vaginosis

Thin fishy-smelling discharge; pH >4.5; clue cells; Gardnerella

Lichen sclerosus

White atrophic plaques; itching, soreness; risk of malignancy; clobetasol

Labial adhesions

Prepubertal common; usually asymptomatic; topical oestrogen if needed

Bartholin's cyst

Blocked duct; presents as swelling; may become abscess; word catheter if recurrent

Vulvodynia

Chronic vulvar pain without cause; multidisciplinary management

Red flags

Persistent bleeding, mass, suspicion of abuse - refer gynaecology

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Vulvovaginitis causesNon-specific (prepubertal), Candida (post-pubertal), bacterial, STI
STI-related organismsChlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas, HSV, HPV
Non-specific vaginitisCommonest in young adolescents; mixed flora; hygiene-related
Candida vulvovaginitisItching, thick white discharge; pH <4.5; treat with clotrimazole/fluconazole
Bacterial vaginosisThin fishy-smelling discharge; pH >4.5; clue cells; Gardnerella
Lichen sclerosusWhite atrophic plaques; itching, soreness; risk of malignancy; clobetasol
Labial adhesionsPrepubertal common; usually asymptomatic; topical oestrogen if needed
Bartholin's cystBlocked duct; presents as swelling; may become abscess; word catheter if recurrent
VulvodyniaChronic vulvar pain without cause; multidisciplinary management
Red flagsPersistent bleeding, mass, suspicion of abuse - refer gynaecology

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Vulvar disorders in adolescents.

Vulvar disorders in adolescents include vulvovaginitis (most common), labial adhesions, lichen sclerosus, vulvodynia, Bartholin's cyst/abscess, and (rarely) malignancy. Vulvovaginitis is usually non-specific (bacterial) in young adolescents and STI-related (chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas) in sexually active adolescents. Presentation: discharge, pruritus, dysuria, soreness, bleeding. Lichen sclerosus presents with porcelain-white atrophic plaques, often with itching and soreness. Labial adhesions are common in young children, less so in adolescents (consider sexual abuse if severe or sudden). Management: hygiene advice, treat specific infection, topical oestrogen for labial adhesions, potent topical steroid for lichen sclerosus.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

Vulvovaginitis causes: Non-specific (prepubertal), Candida (post-pubertal), bacterial, STI. STI-related organisms: Chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas, HSV, HPV. Non-specific vaginitis: Commonest in young adolescents; mixed flora; hygiene-related. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Vulvar disorders in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Candida vulvovaginitis - Itching, thick white discharge; pH <4.5; treat with clotrimazole/fluconazole; Bacterial vaginosis - Thin fishy-smelling discharge; pH >4.5; clue cells; Gardnerella. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

Investigations include: Lichen sclerosus - White atrophic plaques; itching, soreness; risk of malignancy; clobetasol; Labial adhesions - Prepubertal common; usually asymptomatic; topical oestrogen if needed. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

First-line management: Bartholin's cyst - Blocked duct; presents as swelling; may become abscess; word catheter if recurrent; Vulvodynia - Chronic vulvar pain without cause; multidisciplinary management. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Red flags - Persistent bleeding, mass, suspicion of abuse - refer gynaecology. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Vulvar disorders in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

Mind Map

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Vulvovaginitis causes in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Non-specific (prepubertal), Candida (post-pubertal), bacterial, STI

B) Chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas, HSV, HPV

C) Commonest in young adolescents; mixed flora; hygiene-related

D) Itching, thick white discharge; pH <4.5; treat with clotrimazole/fluconazole

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding STI-related organisms in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Commonest in young adolescents; mixed flora; hygiene-related

B) Chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas, HSV, HPV

C) Itching, thick white discharge; pH <4.5; treat with clotrimazole/fluconazole

D) Thin fishy-smelling discharge; pH >4.5; clue cells; Gardnerella

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Non-specific vaginitis in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Itching, thick white discharge; pH <4.5; treat with clotrimazole/fluconazole

B) Thin fishy-smelling discharge; pH >4.5; clue cells; Gardnerella

C) Commonest in young adolescents; mixed flora; hygiene-related

D) White atrophic plaques; itching, soreness; risk of malignancy; clobetasol

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Candida vulvovaginitis in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Thin fishy-smelling discharge; pH >4.5; clue cells; Gardnerella

B) White atrophic plaques; itching, soreness; risk of malignancy; clobetasol

C) Prepubertal common; usually asymptomatic; topical oestrogen if needed

D) Itching, thick white discharge; pH <4.5; treat with clotrimazole/fluconazole

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Bacterial vaginosis in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Thin fishy-smelling discharge; pH >4.5; clue cells; Gardnerella

B) White atrophic plaques; itching, soreness; risk of malignancy; clobetasol

C) Prepubertal common; usually asymptomatic; topical oestrogen if needed

D) Blocked duct; presents as swelling; may become abscess; word catheter if recurre

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Lichen sclerosus in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Prepubertal common; usually asymptomatic; topical oestrogen if needed

B) White atrophic plaques; itching, soreness; risk of malignancy; clobetasol

C) Blocked duct; presents as swelling; may become abscess; word catheter if recurre

D) Chronic vulvar pain without cause; multidisciplinary management

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Labial adhesions in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Blocked duct; presents as swelling; may become abscess; word catheter if recurre

B) Chronic vulvar pain without cause; multidisciplinary management

C) Prepubertal common; usually asymptomatic; topical oestrogen if needed

D) Persistent bleeding, mass, suspicion of abuse - refer gynaecology

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Bartholin's cyst in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Chronic vulvar pain without cause; multidisciplinary management

B) Persistent bleeding, mass, suspicion of abuse - refer gynaecology

C) Non-specific (prepubertal), Candida (post-pubertal), bacterial, STI

D) Blocked duct; presents as swelling; may become abscess; word catheter if recurrent

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Vulvodynia in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Chronic vulvar pain without cause; multidisciplinary management

B) Persistent bleeding, mass, suspicion of abuse - refer gynaecology

C) Non-specific (prepubertal), Candida (post-pubertal), bacterial, STI

D) Chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas, HSV, HPV

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Red flags in Vulvar disorders in adolescents?

A) Non-specific (prepubertal), Candida (post-pubertal), bacterial, STI

B) Persistent bleeding, mass, suspicion of abuse - refer gynaecology

C) Chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas, HSV, HPV

D) Commonest in young adolescents; mixed flora; hygiene-related

Answer: B

▸ 31.8 Adolescent Substance Use 3
▸ 31.8.1 Alcohol And Drug Use In Adolescents

Definition

Adolescent alcohol and drug use is common - by age 15, ~50% have tried alcohol and ~20% cannabis. Most use is experimental and short-lived; ~5-10% develop harmful use patterns or dependency. The developing brain (prefrontal cortex maturation) makes adolescents particularly vulnerable to addiction. Common substances: alcohol, cannabis, MDMA, cocaine, amphetamines, ketamine, nitrous oxide. Management: brief intervention, motivational interviewing, harm reduction (never alone, drink water, test drugs, naloxone for opioids), referral to specialist CAMHS or young persons drug service. Substance use is a safeguarding concern under 18.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

50% of 15 yr-olds have tried alcohol; 20% cannabis; 5-10% harmful use

Common substances

Alcohol, cannabis, MDMA, cocaine, amphetamines, ketamine, nitrous oxide

Vulnerability

Developing brain - prefrontal cortex matures into mid-20s

CRAFFT screening

Car, Relax, Alone, Forget, Friends, Trouble - adolescent substance use screen

Brief intervention

FRAMES: Feedback, Responsibility, Advice, Menu, Empathy, Self-efficacy

Motivational interviewing

Open Qs, reflective listening, elicit change talk, avoid confrontation

Harm reduction

Eat before, pace drinks, water, designated driver, test drugs, naloxone

Specialist referral

CAMHS, young persons drug/alcohol service (e.g., FRANK in UK)

Withdrawal

Alcohol: 6-24 hr onset; delirium tremens 48-96 hr; benzodiazepines: prolonged

Safeguarding

Substance use in <18 is a safeguarding concern; multi-agency assessment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence50% of 15 yr-olds have tried alcohol; 20% cannabis; 5-10% harmful use
Common substancesAlcohol, cannabis, MDMA, cocaine, amphetamines, ketamine, nitrous oxide
VulnerabilityDeveloping brain - prefrontal cortex matures into mid-20s
CRAFFT screeningCar, Relax, Alone, Forget, Friends, Trouble - adolescent substance use screen
Brief interventionFRAMES: Feedback, Responsibility, Advice, Menu, Empathy, Self-efficacy
Motivational interviewingOpen Qs, reflective listening, elicit change talk, avoid confrontation
Harm reductionEat before, pace drinks, water, designated driver, test drugs, naloxone
Specialist referralCAMHS, young persons drug/alcohol service (e.g., FRANK in UK)
WithdrawalAlcohol: 6-24 hr onset; delirium tremens 48-96 hr; benzodiazepines: prolonged
SafeguardingSubstance use in <18 is a safeguarding concern; multi-agency assessment

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Alcohol and drug use in adolescents.

Adolescent alcohol and drug use is common - by age 15, ~50% have tried alcohol and ~20% cannabis. Most use is experimental and short-lived; ~5-10% develop harmful use patterns or dependency. The developing brain (prefrontal cortex maturation) makes adolescents particularly vulnerable to addiction. Common substances: alcohol, cannabis, MDMA, cocaine, amphetamines, ketamine, nitrous oxide. Management: brief intervention, motivational interviewing, harm reduction (never alone, drink water, test drugs, naloxone for opioids), referral to specialist CAMHS or young persons drug service. Substance use is a safeguarding concern under 18.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Alcohol and drug use in adolescents?

Prevalence: 50% of 15 yr-olds have tried alcohol; 20% cannabis; 5-10% harmful use. Common substances: Alcohol, cannabis, MDMA, cocaine, amphetamines, ketamine, nitrous oxide. Vulnerability: Developing brain - prefrontal cortex matures into mid-20s. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Alcohol and drug use in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: CRAFFT screening - Car, Relax, Alone, Forget, Friends, Trouble - adolescent substance use screen; Brief intervention - FRAMES: Feedback, Responsibility, Advice, Menu, Empathy, Self-efficacy. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Alcohol and drug use in adolescents?

Investigations include: Motivational interviewing - Open Qs, reflective listening, elicit change talk, avoid confrontation; Harm reduction - Eat before, pace drinks, water, designated driver, test drugs, naloxone. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Alcohol and drug use in adolescents?

First-line management: Specialist referral - CAMHS, young persons drug/alcohol service (e.g., FRANK in UK); Withdrawal - Alcohol: 6-24 hr onset; delirium tremens 48-96 hr; benzodiazepines: prolonged. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Alcohol and drug use in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Safeguarding - Substance use in <18 is a safeguarding concern; multi-agency assessment. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Alcohol and drug use in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

  2. B - Common substances: Alcohol, cannabis, MDMA, cocaine, amphetamines, ketamine, ni
  3. C - Vulnerability: Developing brain - prefrontal cortex matures into mid-20s
  4. D - CRAFFT screening: Car, Relax, Alone, Forget, Friends, Trouble - adolescent sub
  5. A - Brief intervention: FRAMES: Feedback, Responsibility, Advice, Menu, Empathy, Sel
  6. B - Motivational interviewing: Open Qs, reflective listening, elicit change talk, avoid con
  7. C - Harm reduction: Eat before, pace drinks, water, designated driver, test drug
  8. D - Specialist referral: CAMHS, young persons drug/alcohol service (e.g., FRANK in UK
  9. A - Withdrawal: Alcohol: 6-24 hr onset; delirium tremens 48-96 hr; benzodiaz
  10. B - Safeguarding: Substance use in <18 is a safeguarding concern; multi-agency

SBAs

  1. D - Prevalence: 50% of 15 yr-olds have tried alcohol; 20% cannabis; 5-10% ha
  2. D - Common substances: Alcohol, cannabis, MDMA, cocaine, amphetamines, ketamine, ni
  3. D - Vulnerability: Developing brain - prefrontal cortex matures into mid-20s
  4. D - CRAFFT screening: Car, Relax, Alone, Forget, Friends, Trouble - adolescent sub
  5. D - Brief intervention: FRAMES: Feedback, Responsibility, Advice, Menu, Empathy, Sel
  6. D - Motivational interviewing: Open Qs, reflective listening, elicit change talk, avoid con
  7. D - Harm reduction: Eat before, pace drinks, water, designated driver, test drug
  8. D - Specialist referral: CAMHS, young persons drug/alcohol service (e.g., FRANK in UK
  9. D - Withdrawal: Alcohol: 6-24 hr onset; delirium tremens 48-96 hr; benzodiaz
  10. D - Safeguarding: Substance use in <18 is a safeguarding concern; multi-agency

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 31.8.2 Smoking Vaping And Nicotine

Definition

Tobacco use in adolescents has declined (UK 3% of 15-yr-olds smoke regularly), but vaping/e-cigarettes have surged, with 20-30% of 15-yr-olds having tried vaping. Nicotine is highly addictive, and adolescent brains are particularly susceptible. E-cigarettes deliver high nicotine concentrations; long-term respiratory and cardiovascular harms are emerging. Cannabis is typically smoked with tobacco. Management: brief advice, nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) for dependent smokers, behavioural support, school-based prevention. Vaping-related lung injury (EVALI in US) and popcorn lung (bronchiolitis obliterans) are recognised harms.

Core Clinical Content

Smoking prevalence (UK)

3% of 15-yr-olds regular smokers; 20-30% have tried vaping

Nicotine addiction

High; adolescent brains more susceptible (prefrontal maturation)

Vaping harms

EVALI (US outbreak 2019), popcorn lung (diacetyl), acute lung injury

Cannabis

Usually smoked with tobacco; 20% of 15-yr-olds have tried; linked to psychosis risk

NRT in adolescents

Safe and effective; patches, gum, lozenges; behavioural support first-line

Brief advice

3 As: Ask, Advise, Arrange (referral)

Behavioural support

Motivational interviewing, NRT, school-based programmes

Pharmacotherapy

Varenicline/bupropion not licensed <18; NRT is the option

School-based prevention

Evidence-based programmes; social norms approach

Popcorn lung

Bronchiolitis obliterans from diacetyl; some early reports in vapers

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Smoking prevalence (UK)3% of 15-yr-olds regular smokers; 20-30% have tried vaping
Nicotine addictionHigh; adolescent brains more susceptible (prefrontal maturation)
Vaping harmsEVALI (US outbreak 2019), popcorn lung (diacetyl), acute lung injury
CannabisUsually smoked with tobacco; 20% of 15-yr-olds have tried; linked to psychosis risk
NRT in adolescentsSafe and effective; patches, gum, lozenges; behavioural support first-line
Brief advice3 As: Ask, Advise, Arrange (referral)
Behavioural supportMotivational interviewing, NRT, school-based programmes
PharmacotherapyVarenicline/bupropion not licensed <18; NRT is the option
School-based preventionEvidence-based programmes; social norms approach
Popcorn lungBronchiolitis obliterans from diacetyl; some early reports in vapers

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Smoking, vaping and nicotine.

Tobacco use in adolescents has declined (UK 3% of 15-yr-olds smoke regularly), but vaping/e-cigarettes have surged, with 20-30% of 15-yr-olds having tried vaping. Nicotine is highly addictive, and adolescent brains are particularly susceptible. E-cigarettes deliver high nicotine concentrations; long-term respiratory and cardiovascular harms are emerging. Cannabis is typically smoked with tobacco. Management: brief advice, nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) for dependent smokers, behavioural support, school-based prevention. Vaping-related lung injury (EVALI in US) and popcorn lung (bronchiolitis obliterans) are recognised harms.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Smoking, vaping and nicotine?

Smoking prevalence (UK): 3% of 15-yr-olds regular smokers; 20-30% have tried vaping. Nicotine addiction: High; adolescent brains more susceptible (prefrontal maturation). Vaping harms: EVALI (US outbreak 2019), popcorn lung (diacetyl), acute lung injury. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Smoking, vaping and nicotine recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Cannabis - Usually smoked with tobacco; 20% of 15-yr-olds have tried; linked to psychosis risk; NRT in adolescents - Safe and effective; patches, gum, lozenges; behavioural support first-line. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Smoking, vaping and nicotine?

Investigations include: Brief advice - 3 As: Ask, Advise, Arrange (referral); Behavioural support - Motivational interviewing, NRT, school-based programmes. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Smoking, vaping and nicotine?

First-line management: Pharmacotherapy - Varenicline/bupropion not licensed <18; NRT is the option; School-based prevention - Evidence-based programmes; social norms approach. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Smoking, vaping and nicotine?

Red flags and complications: Popcorn lung - Bronchiolitis obliterans from diacetyl; some early reports in vapers. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Smoking, vaping and nicotine - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 31.8.3 Substance Use Disorder In Adolescents

Definition

Substance use disorder (SUD) in adolescents is defined as a problematic pattern of substance use leading to clinically significant impairment or distress, with DSM-5 criteria (>=2 in 12 months: tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-down, time spent, craving, failure of role obligations, social problems, dangerous use, continued despite problems). Prevalence 5-10% of adolescents. Brain changes from adolescent use are long-lasting. Comorbidity with mental illness, ADHD, trauma is the rule. Management: comprehensive assessment, family involvement, motivational enhancement therapy, CBT, contingency management, residential rehabilitation for severe cases.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 SUD criteria

=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe

Tolerance/withdrawal

Adapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, alcohol

Prevalence

5-10% of adolescents meet criteria for SUD

Comorbidity

ADHD, depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, trauma in >50%

ASSIST/WHO screening

Alcohol, Smoking, Substance Involvement Screening Test

First-line therapy

Motivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT

Family therapy

Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Family Therapy (BSFT)

Contingency management

Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence

Residential rehab

For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month programmes

Outcomes

Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbid disorder treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 SUD criteria>=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe
Tolerance/withdrawalAdapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, alcohol
Prevalence5-10% of adolescents meet criteria for SUD
ComorbidityADHD, depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, trauma in >50%
ASSIST/WHO screeningAlcohol, Smoking, Substance Involvement Screening Test
First-line therapyMotivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT
Family therapyMultidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Family Therapy (BSFT)
Contingency managementVouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence
Residential rehabFor severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month programmes
OutcomesBetter with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbid disorder treatment

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Substance use disorder in adolescents.

Substance use disorder (SUD) in adolescents is defined as a problematic pattern of substance use leading to clinically significant impairment or distress, with DSM-5 criteria (>=2 in 12 months: tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-down, time spent, craving, failure of role obligations, social problems, dangerous use, continued despite problems). Prevalence 5-10% of adolescents. Brain changes from adolescent use are long-lasting. Comorbidity with mental illness, ADHD, trauma is the rule. Management: comprehensive assessment, family involvement, motivational enhancement therapy, CBT, contingency management, residential rehabilitation for severe cases.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Substance use disorder in adolescents?

DSM-5 SUD criteria: >=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe. Tolerance/withdrawal: Adapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, alcohol. Prevalence: 5-10% of adolescents meet criteria for SUD. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Substance use disorder in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Comorbidity - ADHD, depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, trauma in >50%; ASSIST/WHO screening - Alcohol, Smoking, Substance Involvement Screening Test. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Substance use disorder in adolescents?

Investigations include: First-line therapy - Motivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT; Family therapy - Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Family Therapy (BSFT). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Substance use disorder in adolescents?

First-line management: Contingency management - Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence; Residential rehab - For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month programmes. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Substance use disorder in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Outcomes - Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbid disorder treatment. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Substance use disorder in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

D) ADHD, depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, trauma in >50%
