C) Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Family Therapy (BSFT)

D) Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding First-line therapy in Substance use disorder in adolescents?

A) Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Family Therapy (BSFT)

B) Motivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT

C) Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence

D) For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month programmes

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Family therapy in Substance use disorder in adolescents?

A) Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence

B) For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month programmes

C) Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Family Therapy (BSFT)

D) Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbid disorder treatment

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Contingency management in Substance use disorder in adolescents?

A) For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month programmes

B) Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbid disorder treatment

C) >=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe

D) Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Residential rehab in Substance use disorder in adolescents?

A) For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month programmes

B) Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbid disorder treatment

C) >=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe

D) Adapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, alcohol

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Outcomes in Substance use disorder in adolescents?

A) >=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe

B) Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbid disorder treatment

C) Adapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, alcohol

D) 5-10% of adolescents meet criteria for SUD

Answer: B

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding DSM-5 SUD criteria? A) Adapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, B) 5-10% of adolescents meet criteria for SUD C) ADHD, depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, trauma in >50% D) >=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Tolerance/withdrawal? A) 5-10% of adolescents meet criteria for SUD B) ADHD, depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, trauma in >50% C) Alcohol, Smoking, Substance Involvement Screening Test D) Adapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Prevalence? A) ADHD, depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, trauma in >50% B) Alcohol, Smoking, Substance Involvement Screening Test C) Motivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT D) 5-10% of adolescents meet criteria for SUD Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Comorbidity? A) Alcohol, Smoking, Substance Involvement Screening Test B) Motivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT C) Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Fami D) ADHD, depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, trauma in >50% Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding ASSIST/WHO screening? A) Motivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT B) Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Fami C) Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence D) Alcohol, Smoking, Substance Involvement Screening Test Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding First-line therapy? A) Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Fami B) Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence C) For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month D) Motivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Family therapy? A) Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence B) For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month C) Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbi D) Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Fami Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Contingency management? A) For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month B) Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbi C) >=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe D) Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Residential rehab? A) Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbi B) >=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe C) Adapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, D) For severe SUD with failed outpatient treatment; 3-12 month Answer: D
  1. A 16-year-old presents with features consistent with Substance use disorder in adolescents. Which of the following is most appropriate regarding Outcomes? A) >=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe B) Adapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, C) 5-10% of adolescents meet criteria for SUD D) Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbi Answer: D

Flashcards

  • Q: DSM-5 SUD criteria?

A: >=2 of 11 in 12 months; 2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, >=6 severe

  • Q: Tolerance/withdrawal?

A: Adapted for adolescents - may develop quickly with nicotine, alcohol

  • Q: Prevalence?

A: 5-10% of adolescents meet criteria for SUD

  • Q: Comorbidity?

A: ADHD, depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, trauma in >50%

  • Q: ASSIST/WHO screening?

A: Alcohol, Smoking, Substance Involvement Screening Test

  • Q: First-line therapy?

A: Motivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT

  • Q: Family therapy?

A: Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Family Therapy (BSFT)

  • Q: Contingency management?

A: Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  7. C - Family therapy: Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Fami

  10. B - Outcomes: Better with earlier intervention, family engagement, comorbi

  6. D - First-line therapy: Motivational Enhancement Therapy (MET) + CBT
  7. D - Family therapy: Multidimensional Family Therapy (MDFT); Brief Strategic Fami
  8. D - Contingency management: Vouchers/rewards for abstinence - high-quality evidence

▸ 31.9 Chronic Disease and Adherence 2
▸ 31.9.1 Adherence In Adolescents

Definition

Adherence (preferred term to compliance) is the extent to which a persons behaviour corresponds with agreed recommendations from a healthcare provider. Adolescent adherence is often poor (50% non-adherence in chronic conditions) due to developmental factors (risk-taking, peer influence, desire for normality), cognitive factors (poor future orientation), and disease factors (asymptomatic intervals, complex regimens). Consequences: disease progression, treatment failure, resistance (HIV, TB). Strategies: simplified regimens, reminder systems, education, motivational interviewing, family involvement, transition preparation.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

50% non-adherence in adolescent chronic disease; 25% in acute conditions

Adolescent factors

Risk-taking, peer influence, desire for normality, poor future orientation

Disease factors

Asymptomatic intervals, complex regimens, side effects, restricted lifestyle

Measurement

Self-report (overestimates), pharmacy refill, MEMS caps, drug levels (e.g., antiretrovirals)

Morisky scale

8-item validated self-report adherence scale

Strategies

Simplify regimen, once-daily dosing, blister packs, alarms, app reminders

Motivational interviewing

Elicit reasons for change; address ambivalence; family involvement

Consequences of non-adherence

Disease progression, transplant rejection, antiretroviral resistance, death

Informed dissent

Competent adolescent may decline treatment; respect while exploring reasons

Transition prep

Self-management skills (meds, appointments, communication) start in early adolescence

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence50% non-adherence in adolescent chronic disease; 25% in acute conditions
Adolescent factorsRisk-taking, peer influence, desire for normality, poor future orientation
Disease factorsAsymptomatic intervals, complex regimens, side effects, restricted lifestyle
MeasurementSelf-report (overestimates), pharmacy refill, MEMS caps, drug levels (e.g., antiretrovirals)
Morisky scale8-item validated self-report adherence scale
StrategiesSimplify regimen, once-daily dosing, blister packs, alarms, app reminders
Motivational interviewingElicit reasons for change; address ambivalence; family involvement
Consequences of non-adherenceDisease progression, transplant rejection, antiretroviral resistance, death
Informed dissentCompetent adolescent may decline treatment; respect while exploring reasons
Transition prepSelf-management skills (meds, appointments, communication) start in early adolescence

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Adherence in adolescents.

Adherence (preferred term to compliance) is the extent to which a persons behaviour corresponds with agreed recommendations from a healthcare provider. Adolescent adherence is often poor (50% non-adherence in chronic conditions) due to developmental factors (risk-taking, peer influence, desire for normality), cognitive factors (poor future orientation), and disease factors (asymptomatic intervals, complex regimens). Consequences: disease progression, treatment failure, resistance (HIV, TB). Strategies: simplified regimens, reminder systems, education, motivational interviewing, family involvement, transition preparation.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Adherence in adolescents?

Prevalence: 50% non-adherence in adolescent chronic disease; 25% in acute conditions. Adolescent factors: Risk-taking, peer influence, desire for normality, poor future orientation. Disease factors: Asymptomatic intervals, complex regimens, side effects, restricted lifestyle. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Adherence in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Measurement - Self-report (overestimates), pharmacy refill, MEMS caps, drug levels (e.g., antiretrovirals); Morisky scale - 8-item validated self-report adherence scale. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Adherence in adolescents?

Investigations include: Strategies - Simplify regimen, once-daily dosing, blister packs, alarms, app reminders; Motivational interviewing - Elicit reasons for change; address ambivalence; family involvement. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Adherence in adolescents?

First-line management: Consequences of non-adherence - Disease progression, transplant rejection, antiretroviral resistance, death; Informed dissent - Competent adolescent may decline treatment; respect while exploring reasons. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Adherence in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Transition prep - Self-management skills (meds, appointments, communication) start in early adolescence. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adherence in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

Mind Map

Adherence in adolescents
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Recognition
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Examination
├── Investigation
├── Management
│   ├── Acute
│   └── Long-term
└── Complications
    ├── Short-term
    └── Long-term

StepAction
1. RecognitionIdentify key features
2. AssessmentHEEADSSS psychosocial screen + focused exam
3. AcuteStabilise; treat emergencies; safeguarding
4. Long-termMultidisciplinary team; transition planning
5. MonitorMental health, adherence, growth, puberty

▸ 31.9.2 Chronic Disease In Adolescents

Definition

Adolescents with chronic disease (asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, cystic fibrosis, congenital heart disease, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer survivors) face unique challenges: growth/puberty, school absence, peer relationships, body image, transition to adult care, adherence, mental health comorbidity. ~15% of adolescents have a chronic condition. Management is multidisciplinary (paediatric, primary care, mental health, social work, education). Self-management skills should be progressively developed. Ready Steady Go and HEADSS for chronic disease frameworks support structured assessment. Successful transition to adult care is the long-term goal.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

15% of adolescents have a chronic health condition

Common conditions

Asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, CF, CHD, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer

Impact on adolescent

Growth/puberty, school absence, body image, peer relationships, mental health

HEADSS-Chronic

Add questions on: understanding of condition, treatments, healthcare team, future

Multidisciplinary team

Paediatrician, GP, nurse specialist, mental health, social work, school

Mental health comorbidity

2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, particularly in poorly controlled disease

Self-management

Gradual transfer of responsibility from parent to adolescent (Ready Steady Go)

Adherence

Major challenge; see Adherence topic

Sexual health

Counselling on contraception, teratogenicity (e.g., valproate, methotrexate)

Transition planning

Starts ~12-14 yrs; structured handover to adult services (see Transition of Care)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence15% of adolescents have a chronic health condition
Common conditionsAsthma, diabetes, epilepsy, CF, CHD, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer
Impact on adolescentGrowth/puberty, school absence, body image, peer relationships, mental health
HEADSS-ChronicAdd questions on: understanding of condition, treatments, healthcare team, future
Multidisciplinary teamPaediatrician, GP, nurse specialist, mental health, social work, school
Mental health comorbidity2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, particularly in poorly controlled disease
Self-managementGradual transfer of responsibility from parent to adolescent (Ready Steady Go)
AdherenceMajor challenge; see Adherence topic
Sexual healthCounselling on contraception, teratogenicity (e.g., valproate, methotrexate)
Transition planningStarts ~12-14 yrs; structured handover to adult services (see Transition of Care)

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Chronic disease in adolescents.

Adolescents with chronic disease (asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, cystic fibrosis, congenital heart disease, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer survivors) face unique challenges: growth/puberty, school absence, peer relationships, body image, transition to adult care, adherence, mental health comorbidity. ~15% of adolescents have a chronic condition. Management is multidisciplinary (paediatric, primary care, mental health, social work, education). Self-management skills should be progressively developed. Ready Steady Go and HEADSS for chronic disease frameworks support structured assessment. Successful transition to adult care is the long-term goal.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Chronic disease in adolescents?

Prevalence: 15% of adolescents have a chronic health condition. Common conditions: Asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, CF, CHD, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer. Impact on adolescent: Growth/puberty, school absence, body image, peer relationships, mental health. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Chronic disease in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: HEADSS-Chronic - Add questions on: understanding of condition, treatments, healthcare team, future; Multidisciplinary team - Paediatrician, GP, nurse specialist, mental health, social work, school. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Chronic disease in adolescents?

Investigations include: Mental health comorbidity - 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, particularly in poorly controlled disease; Self-management - Gradual transfer of responsibility from parent to adolescent (Ready Steady Go). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Chronic disease in adolescents?

First-line management: Adherence - Major challenge; see Adherence topic; Sexual health - Counselling on contraception, teratogenicity (e.g., valproate, methotrexate). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Chronic disease in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Transition planning - Starts ~12-14 yrs; structured handover to adult services (see Transition of Care). Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Chronic disease in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

Mind Map

Chronic disease in adolescents
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Recognition
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Examination
├── Investigation
├── Management
│   ├── Acute
│   └── Long-term
└── Complications
    ├── Short-term
    └── Long-term

One-Page Revision Card

StepAction
1. RecognitionIdentify key features
2. AssessmentHEEADSSS psychosocial screen + focused exam
3. AcuteStabilise; treat emergencies; safeguarding
4. Long-termMultidisciplinary team; transition planning
5. MonitorMental health, adherence, growth, puberty

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Prevalence in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) 15% of adolescents have a chronic health condition

B) Asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, CF, CHD, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer

C) Growth/puberty, school absence, body image, peer relationships, mental health

D) Add questions on: understanding of condition, treatments, healthcare team, futur

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Common conditions in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) Growth/puberty, school absence, body image, peer relationships, mental health

B) Asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, CF, CHD, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer

C) Add questions on: understanding of condition, treatments, healthcare team, futur

D) Paediatrician, GP, nurse specialist, mental health, social work, school

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Impact on adolescent in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) Add questions on: understanding of condition, treatments, healthcare team, futur

B) Paediatrician, GP, nurse specialist, mental health, social work, school

C) Growth/puberty, school absence, body image, peer relationships, mental health

D) 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, particularly in poorly controlled disease

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding HEADSS-Chronic in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) Paediatrician, GP, nurse specialist, mental health, social work, school

B) 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, particularly in poorly controlled disease

C) Gradual transfer of responsibility from parent to adolescent (Ready Steady Go)

D) Add questions on: understanding of condition, treatments, healthcare team, future

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Multidisciplinary team in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) Paediatrician, GP, nurse specialist, mental health, social work, school

B) 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, particularly in poorly controlled disease

C) Gradual transfer of responsibility from parent to adolescent (Ready Steady Go)

D) Major challenge; see Adherence topic

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Mental health comorbidity in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) Gradual transfer of responsibility from parent to adolescent (Ready Steady Go)

B) 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, particularly in poorly controlled disease

C) Major challenge; see Adherence topic

D) Counselling on contraception, teratogenicity (e.g., valproate, methotrexate)

Answer: B

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Self-management in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) Major challenge; see Adherence topic

B) Counselling on contraception, teratogenicity (e.g., valproate, methotrexate)

C) Gradual transfer of responsibility from parent to adolescent (Ready Steady Go)

D) Starts ~12-14 yrs; structured handover to adult services (see Transition of Care

Answer: C

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Adherence in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) Counselling on contraception, teratogenicity (e.g., valproate, methotrexate)

B) Starts ~12-14 yrs; structured handover to adult services (see Transition of Care

C) 15% of adolescents have a chronic health condition

D) Major challenge; see Adherence topic

Answer: D

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Sexual health in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) Counselling on contraception, teratogenicity (e.g., valproate, methotrexate)

B) Starts ~12-14 yrs; structured handover to adult services (see Transition of Care

C) 15% of adolescents have a chronic health condition

D) Asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, CF, CHD, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true regarding Transition planning in Chronic disease in adolescents?

A) 15% of adolescents have a chronic health condition

B) Starts ~12-14 yrs; structured handover to adult services (see Transition of Care)

C) Asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, CF, CHD, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer

D) Growth/puberty, school absence, body image, peer relationships, mental health

Answer: B

Flashcards

  • Q: Prevalence?

A: 15% of adolescents have a chronic health condition

  • Q: Common conditions?

A: Asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, CF, CHD, IBD, JIA, CKD, sickle cell, cancer

  • Q: Impact on adolescent?

A: Growth/puberty, school absence, body image, peer relationships, mental health

  • Q: HEADSS-Chronic?

A: Add questions on: understanding of condition, treatments, healthcare team, future

  • Q: Multidisciplinary team?

A: Paediatrician, GP, nurse specialist, mental health, social work, school

  • Q: Mental health comorbidity?

A: 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, particularly in poorly controlled disease

  • Q: Self-management?

A: Gradual transfer of responsibility from parent to adolescent (Ready Steady Go)

  • Q: Adherence?

A: Major challenge; see Adherence topic
